topics.title: Basics of Computer Graphics and its Applications. Video Display Devices - Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT602 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Computer Graphics and its Applications. Video Display Devices - Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computer Graphics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Computer Graphics (CG)** is a discipline of Computer Science that deals with the generation, representation, manipulation, storage, and display of pictorial (graphical) data using a digital computer. It involves the use of specialized hardware (display devices, GPUs) and software (algorithms, APIs like OpenGL/DirectX) to create and handle visual content ranging from 2D line drawings to photorealistic 3D animated scenes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Artist's Toolbox"

Imagine you are a painter who has been given a digital canvas instead of a physical one. The computer is your infinite palette of colors, the algorithms are your brushes, and the display device (CRT/LCD) is your canvas on which the image finally appears. The artist (you/programmer) gives instructions like *"draw a line from point A to B"* or *"fill this region with red"*. Computer Graphics is precisely this — the science of telling a computer *what* to draw, *how* to draw it, and *where* to draw it, using mathematical precision.

---

## 1.2 Sub-Areas of Computer Graphics (KTU Syllabus Coverage)

| Sub-Area | What it Handles | Real-World Example |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| **2D Graphics** | Flat drawings, diagrams, line art | Blueprints, CAD drawings |
| **3D Graphics** | Three-dimensional scenes with depth | Animated movies (Pixar) |
| **Image Processing** | Manipulation of existing raster images | Instagram filters, MRI scans |
| **Visualization** | Converting scientific data to visual form | Weather maps, CT scans |
| **GUI Design** | Graphical User Interfaces | Windows/macOS interfaces |
| **Virtual Reality (VR)** | Immersive 3D interactive environments | Oculus Rift games |
| **Augmented Reality (AR)** | Overlaying graphics on real-world view | Pokemon GO, surgical navigation |

---

## 1.3 Applications of Computer Graphics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield List (Frequently Asked in ESE)**
> 1. **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) / CAM** — designing circuits, automobiles, aircraft.
> 2. **Presentation Graphics** — bar charts, line graphs in business reports.
> 3. **Entertainment** — movies, video games, animated films.
> 4. **Education & Training** — flight simulators, surgical simulators.
> 5. **Scientific Visualization** — molecular models, astrophysical simulations.
> 6. **Image Processing** — medical imaging (X-ray enhancement), satellite imagery.
> 7. **GUI** — every modern operating system.

---

## 1.4 Video Display Devices — The Output Hardware

A **video display device** is the electronic component responsible for presenting the visual output generated by the computer to the user. Display devices are broadly classified as:

1. **Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes (CRT)** — *Primary focus of this module.*
2. **Raster-Scan Displays** (CRT-based but using frame buffers).
3. **Random-Scan Displays** (Vector displays).
4. **Direct-View Storage Tubes (DVST)**.
5. **Flat-Panel Displays** — LCD, LED, OLED, Plasma.
6. **3D Displays** — Stereoscopic and Autostereoscopic systems.
7. **Head-Mounted Displays (HMD)** — VR headsets.

---

## 1.5 Refresh Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) — The Core Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** is a specialized vacuum tube in which one or more electron beams, emitted from an electron gun, are accelerated, focused, and deflected onto a phosphor-coated screen. The phosphor emits visible light when struck by the electrons, producing the image. The image must be **refreshed** (redrawn) many times per second to maintain a stable, flicker-free picture — hence the name **Refresh CRT**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Painter Robot"

Think of a CRT as a tiny, hyper-fast robotic painter hidden inside the back of your monitor. This robot:
- Holds a single glowing "brush tip" (the electron beam) that can paint *only one dot* at a time.
- Moves the brush with extreme speed across a blank canvas (the phosphor screen) from left to right, line by line, top to bottom.
- After painting the entire canvas in about **1/60th of a second**, it immediately returns to the top-left corner and starts over.
- Because the dots of paint (phosphor glow) fade very quickly, the robot must continuously repaint the canvas. This continuous repainting is called **refreshing**.

---

## 1.6 Essential Display Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

- **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable point of light on the screen.
- **Resolution:** Total number of distinct pixels that can be displayed in each dimension (e.g., **1920 × 1080**).
- **Aspect Ratio:** Ratio of width to height of the displayable area (e.g., **4:3**, **16:9**).
- **Refresh Rate (Frequency):** Number of times per second the screen is redrawn, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Frame Buffer (Video RAM / VRAM):** A dedicated memory area in the display hardware that holds the pixel intensity/color values of the entire screen.
- **Phosphor Persistence:** The time (usually in milliseconds) for which a phosphor dot continues to glow after the electron beam has passed.
- **Flicker:** Visible闪烁 caused when the refresh rate is too low for the persistence of the phosphor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Construction of a Refresh CRT

A CRT is essentially a sealed **glass vacuum tube** with the following major subsystems:

### A. Electron Gun Assembly (located in the neck)
1. **Cathode (Heated Filament):** Heated to high temperature to emit electrons via **thermionic emission**.
2. **Control Grid:** A negatively charged cylinder surrounding the cathode that controls the *intensity* (brightness) of the electron beam by varying the voltage applied to it. Higher negative voltage → fewer electrons → dimmer pixel.
3. **Focusing System:** Anodes (positive electrodes) that converge the diverging electrons into a narrow, sharp beam.
4. **Accelerating Anode:** Maintains a high positive potential (typically **25 kV to 50 kV**) to accelerate electrons towards the screen.

### B. Deflection System
1. **Electromagnetic Deflection Yoke:** A pair of coils (one horizontal, one vertical) wrapped around the neck of the CRT. When current passes through, it creates a magnetic field that **bends** the electron beam, allowing it to strike any position on the screen.
2. Two pairs are required:
   - **Horizontal Deflection Coils** — move beam left ↔ right.
   - **Vertical Deflection Coils** — move beam up ↕ down.

### C. Phosphor Screen
- The interior of the screen face is coated with a thin layer of **phosphor** (e.g., Zinc Sulphide, Europium-activated Yttrium compounds).
- When high-energy electrons strike the phosphor, electrons in the phosphor atoms are excited to higher energy levels and, on returning to ground state, release energy as **visible light** — this phenomenon is called **fluorescence**.
- The residual glow after the beam passes is called **phosphorescence** (governed by persistence).

### D. Glass Envelope
- Funnel-shaped, evacuated to near-vacuum so electrons travel unimpeded.
- The wide end is the viewing screen; the narrow end holds the electron gun.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Electron Emission:** The heated cathode releases free electrons (thermionic emission).
2. **Step 2 — Acceleration:** The accelerating anode's high positive potential pulls electrons forward, giving them high kinetic energy.
3. **Step 3 — Focusing:** The focusing anodes squeeze the beam to a fine point (typically **0.2 mm** diameter on the screen).
4. **Step 4 — Intensity Modulation:** The control grid varies the beam current → varying brightness of the resulting spot.
5. **Step 5 — Deflection:** The yoke's magnetic fields sweep the beam to the desired (x, y) coordinates on the screen.
6. **Step 6 — Phosphor Excitation:** Electrons strike the phosphor, which emits light proportional to beam intensity.
7. **Step 7 — Refresh:** The whole process repeats 60–85 times per second so the eye perceives a steady image.

---

## 2.3 Two Methods of Refreshing a CRT

### Method 1 — Raster-Scan Display (Dominant Technology)
- The beam sweeps the screen in a fixed pattern of horizontal lines from **top to bottom**, left to right.
- Each horizontal pass is called a **scan line**.
- After completing one full screen (a **frame**), the beam returns to the top — this is called **vertical retrace**.
- At the end of each scan line, the beam returns to start the next — **horizontal retrace**.
- The image is stored as a pattern of bits in the **frame buffer**, where each bit (or group of bits) corresponds to one pixel's color/intensity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raster-Scan Refresh Rate Concept**
> To avoid flicker, the frame buffer must be repainted at a rate above the **flicker fusion threshold** of human vision (~50–60 Hz).

#### Interlaced vs. Non-Interlaced (Progressive) Scanning

| Feature | Non-Interlaced (Progressive) | Interlaced |
|---------|------------------------------|------------|
| **Frame division** | All lines drawn in one pass | Frame split into two **fields** (odd & even lines) |
| **Refresh rate** | Full frame per refresh (e.g., 60 Hz) | Half frame per refresh (e.g., 60 fields/s ≈ 30 full frames/s) |
| **Flicker** | Higher quality, less flicker | Reduces bandwidth, but introduces slight flicker |
| **Use case** | Modern monitors, HDTV | Older broadcast TV (NTSC, PAL) |

### Method 2 — Random-Scan (Vector / Calligraphic) Display
- The electron beam is moved directly between endpoints of graphical primitives (lines, curves) **without** following a fixed scan pattern.
- Only the parts of the screen where something is drawn get illuminated.
- Stores display as a list of **line-drawing commands** in the **display list** (or **refresh display file**).
- Used historically in oscilloscopes and early CAD terminals.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot realistically display shaded regions or full-color photographic content.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU ESE numericals.

### Fundamental Equations of CRT Displays

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Aspect Ratio** | $$AR = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{\text{Horizontal pixels}}{\text{Vertical pixels}}$$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | **Total Resolution** | $$N = N_x \times N_y$$ | $N_x$ = horizontal pixels, $N_y$ = vertical pixels |
| 3 | **Frame Buffer Size** | $$\text{Buffer} = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot b \;\; \text{bits}$$ | $b$ = bits per pixel |
| 4 | **Number of Colors / Intensities** | $$C = 2^{b}$$ | $b$ = bits per pixel (color depth) |
| 5 | **Refresh Cycle Time** | $$T_{refresh} = \frac{1}{f_{refresh}}$$ | $f_{refresh}$ in Hz |
| 6 | **Phosphor Persistence Bound (Anti-Flicker)** | $$T_{persist} \le \frac{1}{f_{refresh}}$$ | Ensures glow does not fade before next refresh |
| 7 | **Horizontal Scan Frequency** | $$f_{h} = N_y \times f_{refresh}$$ | $N_y$ = total scan lines per frame |
| 8 | **Bandwidth Required** | $$BW = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot b \cdot f_{refresh}\;\; \text{bits/sec}$$ | Display channel capacity |
| 9 | **Electron Beam Kinetic Energy** | $$E_{k} = eV = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$ | $V$ = accelerating voltage, $e$ = electron charge, $m$ = electron mass |
| 10 | **Electron Velocity at Anode** | $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}$$ | Derived from kinetic energy equation |

---

### Critical Reminder — Avoiding Markdown Pipe Conflicts

> [!NOTE]
> All mathematical absolute values and conditional logic in this sheet use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert V \vert$) instead of the raw `\|` symbol so that KTU notes rendered in GitHub-flavored Markdown do not break the table grid.

---

## 2.5 Types of Color CRTs (KTU Examinable Variants)

### (a) Beam-Penetration CRT
- Uses **two layers** of phosphor (typically red and green).
- The color depends on the **penetration depth** of the electron beam, controlled by the accelerating voltage.
- **Low voltage** → beam stops at outer (red) phosphor → red color.
- **High voltage** → beam penetrates deeper to green phosphor → green color.
- Only produces **4 colors**: red, green, orange, yellow.
- **Limited color range**, cheap; used in simple industrial displays.

### (b) Shadow-Mask CRT (The Dominant Color CRT — e.g., Trinitron)
- Has **three electron guns** (one each for Red, Green, Blue — RGB).
- Behind the phosphor screen sits a thin metal plate called the **shadow mask**, perforated with thousands of tiny holes.
- Each hole aligns one **triad** of phosphor dots: R, G, B.
- The three beams, slightly offset, converge at a single hole so that each beam strikes only its corresponding colored phosphor dot.
- Produces millions of colors through **additive color mixing**.
- Resolution is limited by the mask dot pitch.

### Quick Comparison

| Feature | Beam-Penetration | Shadow-Mask |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Color variety | 4 colors | 16.7 million+ (24-bit) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Picture quality | Poor | Excellent |
| Phosphor layers | 2 | 3 (RGB) |
| Voltage dependence | Yes (color by voltage) | No |
| Modern usage | Almost obsolete | Dominant in pre-LCD era CRTs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Colors from Bit-Depth

### Problem Statement
Show that if a frame buffer allocates $b$ bits per pixel, the total number of distinct colors or grayscale intensities that can be represented is $C = 2^{b}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Understand the pixel storage model.**
Each pixel in the frame buffer is stored as a binary code of $b$ bits. For $b = 1$ bit, the pixel can take either of $2$ states (commonly 0 = OFF, 1 = ON), giving us two distinct values.

**Step 2 — Generalize using the counting principle of combinatorics.**
Each of the $b$ bit-positions in the pixel code is **independent** and can take **2** possible values (0 or 1).

By the multiplication principle of counting, the total number of distinct bit-patterns that can be formed is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{b \text{ times}} \\
  &= 2^{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
Each unique $b$-bit pattern corresponds to one unique color/intensity setting for that pixel.

Therefore, the number of colors that the display can produce is:

$$
\boxed{C = 2^{b}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
If the frame buffer stores $8$ bits per pixel (true-color single-channel grayscale):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{8} \\
  &= 256 \text{ distinct grayscale shades.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 24-bit RGB color system ($8$ bits per channel × $3$ channels):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{24} \\
  &= 16,\!777,\!216 \text{ distinct colors.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Frame-Buffer Memory Size

### Problem Statement
A raster display has a resolution of $1024 \times 768$ pixels, with each pixel stored in $24$ bits (true color). Derive the minimum frame-buffer memory required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- Horizontal resolution: $N_x = 1024$ pixels.
- Vertical resolution: $N_y = 768$ pixels.
- Color depth: $b = 24$ bits/pixel.

**Step 2 — Compute the total number of pixels.**
The total pixel count $N$ is the product of horizontal and vertical resolutions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= N_x \times N_y \\
  &= 1024 \times 768 \\
  &= 786,\!432 \text{ pixels.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by bits per pixel.**
The total memory requirement in bits is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory (bits)} &= N \times b \\
                      &= 786,\!432 \times 24 \\
                      &= 18,\!874,\!368 \text{ bits.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes.**
Divide by $8$ to convert bits to bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory (bytes)} &= \frac{18,\!874,\!368}{8} \\
                      &= 2,\!359,\!296 \text{ bytes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to megabytes (MB).**
Using $1\,\text{MB} = 1024 \times 1024 = 1,\!048,\!576$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory (MB)} &= \frac{2,\!359,\!296}{1,\!048,\!576} \\
                   &\approx 2.25 \text{ MB.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Frame Buffer Required} \approx 2.25 \text{ MB.}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Display Bandwidth Requirement

### Problem Statement
Compute the bandwidth required for a display system with resolution $1920 \times 1080$, 24-bit color, and a refresh rate of $60$ Hz.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use the bandwidth equation.**

$$
BW = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot b \cdot f_{refresh}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \times 60 \\
   &= (1920 \times 1080) \times (24 \times 60) \\
   &= 2,\!073,\!600 \times 1440 \\
   &= 2,\!985,\!984,\!000 \text{ bits/sec.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Gigabits per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= \frac{2,\!985,\!984,\!000}{10^{9}} \\
   &\approx 2.99 \text{ Gbps.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{BW \approx 2.99 \text{ Gbps} \;\;(\approx 0.374 \text{ GB/s})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Electron Velocity inside a CRT

### Problem Statement
A CRT has an accelerating voltage of $V = 25,\!000$ V. Find the velocity of electrons as they strike the phosphor screen.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy conservation.**
The work done by the electric field on an electron of charge $e$ equals its kinetic energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
eV &= \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v^{2} &= \frac{2eV}{m} \\
v &= \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute constants.**
Electron charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, electron mass $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $V = 25,\!000$ V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25,\!000}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} \\
  &= \sqrt{\frac{8.01 \times 10^{-15}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} \\
  &= \sqrt{8.79 \times 10^{15}} \\
  &\approx 9.38 \times 10^{7} \;\; \text{m/s}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the speed of light.**
Speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v}{c} &= \frac{9.38 \times 10^{7}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \\
            &\approx 0.313.
\end{aligned}
$$

So the electrons travel at roughly **31.3 % of the speed of light**.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{v \approx 9.38 \times 10^{7} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.313\,c.}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Subsystems to Functions

| Subsystem | Physical Principle | Engineering Function | KTU Keyword |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| Heated Cathode | Thermionic Emission | Source of electrons | "Electron source" |
| Control Grid | Electrostatic Repulsion | Beam intensity control | "Z-axis modulation" |
| Focusing Anodes | Electrostatic Lensing | Beam collimation | "Focus electrode" |
| Accelerating Anode | High Potential Difference | Velocity gain | "Acceleration" |
| Deflection Yoke | Electromagnetic Induction (Lorentz Force) | X–Y positioning | "Beam steering" |
| Phosphor Layer | Fluorescence & Phosphorescence | Light generation | "Phosphorescence" |
| Glass Envelope | Vacuum Sealing | Electron pathway integrity | "Vacuum tube" |
| Shadow Mask | Geometric Aperture Selection | Color separation in CRT | "Triad alignment" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Refresh CRT

The diagram below illustrates the complete signal and physical flow inside a refresh CRT system, from frame buffer to phosphor excitation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU or Graphics Processor] --> B[Graphics Card GPU]
    B --> C[Frame Buffer Video RAM]
    C --> D[Video Controller]
    D --> E[Digital to Analog Converter]
    E --> F[Electron Gun Cathode]
    F --> G[Control Grid Intensity Control]
    G --> H[Focusing Anodes]
    H --> I[Accelerating Anode 25 to 50 kV]
    I --> J[Electron Beam]
    J --> K[Deflection Yoke H and V Coils]
    K --> L[Phosphor Coated Screen]
    L --> M[Visible Light Image]
    D -.Horizontal Sync.-> K
    D -.Vertical Sync.-> K
    subgraph feedback[Retrace and Refresh Loop]
        K --> N[Horizontal Retrace]
        K --> O[Vertical Retrace]
        N --> J
        O --> J
    end
    style A fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#003366
    style M fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#806000
    style J fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style K fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
```

---

## 4.2 Raster-Scan vs. Random-Scan Comparison (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Display Scan Type] --> B{Application Domain}
    B -->|Photorealistic Image or Video| C[Raster Scan Display]
    B -->|Vector Diagrams, CAD, Oscilloscope| D[Random Scan Display]
    C --> E[Stores pixels in Frame Buffer]
    D --> F[Stores commands in Display List]
    E --> G[Refresh: top to bottom scan lines]
    F --> H[Refresh: redraw lines sequentially]
    G --> I[Output: Pixels illuminated on phosphor]
    H --> J[Output: Only drawn segments illuminated]
    style A fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style I fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
    style J fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
```

```mermaid
    M -->|Filtered| G[Green Phosphor Dot]

    G -.Adjacent.-> Bc

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Computer Graphics. List any four major applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Computer Graphics is the discipline concerned with the generation, representation, manipulation, and display of graphical data using computers. It uses specialized hardware (GPUs, displays) and software (rendering algorithms) to produce visual content.

Four major applications:
1. **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — engineering and architectural drafting.
2. **Entertainment** — movies, video games, animation.
3. **Medical Imaging** — MRI/CT visualization and enhancement.
4. **Education & Training** — flight simulators, virtual labs.

*(Examiner's note: A clean two-line definition + four crisp application names = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Refresh Cathode Ray Tube? Explain the term "refresh rate".**

**Model Answer:**
A **Refresh CRT** is a vacuum-tube-based display device in which an electron beam, generated by an electron gun, is focused and deflected onto a phosphor-coated screen to produce images. Because the phosphor glow fades quickly, the image must be redrawn repeatedly to appear stable.

**Refresh rate** is the number of times per second the display redraws (refreshes) the entire frame, measured in Hertz (Hz). A typical refresh rate to avoid flicker is **60 Hz or higher**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**a)** Explain the construction and working of a Refresh CRT with a neatly labelled block diagram. (7 Marks)

**b)** Compare Raster-Scan and Random-Scan displays in detail. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction:**
A Refresh CRT consists of the following components:
1. **Electron Gun** — comprises a heated cathode (filament), control grid, focusing anode, and accelerating anode. The cathode emits electrons via thermionic emission.
2. **Deflection System** — electromagnetic yoke coils around the CRT neck.
3. **Phosphor-Coated Screen** — inner face of the screen.
4. **Glass Envelope** — evacuated funnel-shaped tube.

**Working Principle (7 logical steps):**

**Step 1 — Electron Emission:**
The filament heats the cathode; free electrons are emitted via **thermionic emission**.

**Step 2 — Intensity Control:**
The **control grid**, held at a small negative voltage relative to the cathode, regulates the number of electrons passing through. Higher negative voltage reduces brightness (Z-axis modulation).

**Step 3 — Focusing:**
The **focusing anode** uses electrostatic fields to converge the diverging electrons into a narrow, pencil-thin beam.

**Step 4 — Acceleration:**
The **accelerating anode** at a high positive potential (**25 kV – 50 kV**) accelerates the focused electrons towards the screen.

**Step 5 — Deflection:**
The **deflection yoke** produces time-varying magnetic fields; the beam is steered to any (x, y) coordinate on the screen. Horizontal coils move it left-right; vertical coils move it up-down.

**Step 6 — Phosphor Excitation:**
When the high-velocity electrons strike the phosphor, the phosphor atoms get excited and emit visible light — this is **fluorescence**. The afterglow is **phosphorescence**, whose duration equals the **persistence** of the phosphor.

**Step 7 — Continuous Refresh:**
Because the phosphor glow decays within a few milliseconds, the entire image must be **repainted at least 50–60 times per second** to avoid flicker — hence the term *Refresh CRT*.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | Raster-Scan Display | Random-Scan Display |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Beam motion** | Fixed pattern (left→right, top→bottom) | Direct between endpoints |
| **Image storage** | Frame buffer (pixel array) | Display list (line-drawing commands) |
| **Best for** | Realistic images, shading, video | Line drawings, CAD |
| **Color capability** | Excellent (millions of colors) | Limited (mostly monochrome) |
| **Flicker problem** | Yes (needs refresh ≥ 60 Hz) | Minimal (beam only redraws lines) |
| **Resolution** | Limited by pixel density | High for line drawings |
| **Cost** | Higher (needs VRAM) | Lower |
| **Modern relevance** | Standard until LCD takeover | Mostly obsolete |
| **Examples** | Old TVs, computer monitors | Oscilloscopes, early CAD |

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**a)** Describe the construction and working of a Shadow-Mask CRT for color display. Explain how it produces a full range of colors. (7 Marks)

**b)** A raster display system has a resolution of $1280 \times 1024$ pixels with $24$ bits per pixel. Calculate (i) the size of the frame buffer in MB, and (ii) the number of distinct colors it can display. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction of Shadow-Mask CRT:**

A shadow-mask CRT contains:
1. **Three electron guns** — one each for **Red (R)**, **Green (G)**, and **Blue (B)**.
2. A **shadow mask** — a thin metal plate positioned just behind the phosphor screen, perforated with thousands of tiny circular holes arranged in a triangular pattern.
3. A **phosphor screen** coated with triads of phosphor dots — one red, one green, one blue — corresponding to each mask hole.

**Working Principle:**

**Step 1 — Beam Generation:**
Three separate electron beams (R, G, B) are generated by the three electron guns simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Convergence at Mask Hole:**
The three beams are slightly angled so they converge precisely at one hole of the shadow mask.

**Step 3 — Beam Filtering:**
The mask allows only the correctly-angled beam to pass through each hole. Misaligned beams are blocked — this is why the plate is called a "shadow" mask.

**Step 4 — Phosphor Triad Illumination:**
The passing R beam strikes only the red phosphor dot; the G beam only strikes the green dot; the B beam only the blue dot.

**Step 5 — Additive Color Mixing:**
By independently varying the intensities of the three beams for each pixel triad, any color in the visible spectrum can be synthesized through **additive color mixing**. For example:
- R + G = Yellow
- R + B = Magenta
- G + B = Cyan
- R + G + B = White
- No beams = Black

**Step 6 — Number of Colors:**
With 8-bit intensity resolution per channel: $2^{8} \times 2^{8} \times 2^{8} = 2^{24} = 16.7$ million colors.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Frame Buffer Size:**

Total pixels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 1280 \times 1024 \\
  &= 1,\!310,\!720 \text{ pixels.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits} &= N \times 24 \\
            &= 1,\!310,\!720 \times 24 \\
            &= 31,\!457,\!280 \text{ bits.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes} &= \frac{31,\!457,\!280}{8} \\
             &= 3,\!932,\!160 \text{ bytes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to MB (divide by $1,\!048,\!576$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MB} &= \frac{3,\!932,\!160}{1,\!048,\!576} \\
         &\approx 3.75 \text{ MB.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Frame Buffer Size} \approx 3.75 \text{ MB}}
$$

**(ii) Number of Distinct Colors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{24} \\
  &= 16,\!777,\!216 \text{ colors.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 16,\!777,\!216 \text{ distinct colors (True Color)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the conversion chain in buffer problems:** Students frequently leave the answer in bits and forget to divide by 8 to get bytes, then by $1024^2$ to get MB. Partial marks are awarded only if all three stages are explicitly shown.
> 2. **Confusing interlaced refresh rates with full-frame rates:** An "interlaced 60 Hz" display actually refreshes **60 fields/sec ≈ 30 full frames/sec** — a frequent trap.
> 3. **Mixing up shadow-mask and beam-penetration CRTs:** Beam-penetration is **two-layer** and **only 4 colors**; shadow-mask is **three-gun** and **millions of colors**.
> 4. **Missing units in answers:** Always state MB, Gbps, m/s, etc. Unanswered units cause ½ mark deductions.
> 5. **Diagrams in Part B (a):** Examiners award **at least 1 mark** for a properly labelled diagram. Skipping the CRT block diagram is a guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Computer Graphics** = generation + display of images via computers.
- **CRT** = vacuum tube using electron beams on phosphor.
- **Core CRT parts**: Cathode, Control Grid, Focusing Anode, Accelerating Anode, Deflection Yoke, Phosphor Screen.
- **Working chain**: Heat → Emit → Focus → Accelerate → Deflect → Strike Phosphor → Light.
- **Electron beam steering** uses magnetic deflection (Lorentz force: $F = qvB$).
- **Phosphor emits** visible light via **fluorescence** (instant) and **phosphorescence** (afterglow).
- **Refresh rate** = Hz; flicker-free requires ≥ 60 Hz typically.
- **Frame Buffer** = pixel memory in VRAM.
- **Resolution** = $N_x \times N_y$ pixels.
- **Buffer size** = $N_x \cdot N_y \cdot b$ bits.
- **Colors** = $2^{b}$.
- **Bandwidth** = $N_x \cdot N_y \cdot b \cdot f_{refresh}$ bits/sec.
- **Two scan methods**: Raster (line-by-line, pixel-based) vs. Random (vector-based, command list).
- **Interlaced** = two fields per frame; **Non-interlaced** = full frame per refresh.
- **Beam-Penetration CRT** → 4 colors, voltage-controlled penetration depth.
- **Shadow-Mask CRT** → 3 guns (R,G,B) + mask with holes + triads → millions of colors.
- **Persistence** of phosphor must be ≤ $1/f_{refresh}$ to prevent flicker.
- **Real-world heritage**: CRTs dominated until ~2005; now obsolete, replaced by LCD/LED/OLED.
- **Key constants**: Electron charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; mass $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.
- **Typical accelerating voltage** in CRTs: **25 kV – 50 kV**.
- **Aspect ratios**: traditional **4:3**, modern widescreen **16:9**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Scan Displays and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT602 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Scan Displays and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Random Scan Display** (also called **Vector Display**, **Stroke-Writing Display**, or **Calligraphic Display**) is a CRT-based display system in which the electron beam is directed to draw picture **component-by-component** (typically line-by-line) in any arbitrary order, refreshing the entire screen periodically by re-tracing the primitives stored in the **Display File (Refresh Buffer)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The term "Random" in Random Scan refers to the **unsystematic, non-sequential order** in which the electron beam moves to the next endpoint — it does NOT refer to randomness in image content. The beam can jump from drawing a polygon in one corner to a line in the opposite corner without following a fixed scan-line pattern.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **calligrapher writing on a whiteboard with a pen**:
- You don't draw every horizontal line from top to bottom (that would be a Raster Scan, like a TV).
- Instead, you draw each **letter or shape one at a time**, picking up the pen only when needed, in the most efficient order to complete the drawing.
- To keep the picture visible, you must **re-write the whole thing every few seconds** (because ink fades), or the picture disappears.

That "pick-up-and-draw-where-needed" approach is exactly how a **Random Scan Display** works — the electron beam is your pen, and the display file is your calligraphy plan.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Refresh Rate (typical) | **30 – 60 Hz** | cycles/second |
| Deflection Rate (max) | **1 MHz** (for high-end systems) | points/second |
| Beam positioning time | **10 µs** typical | microseconds |
| Display file storage | Vector list, op-codes, coordinates | word-addressable memory |
| Phosphor persistence | **Short persistence** (P31 green) | microseconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphor Persistence** in random scan CRTs is intentionally **short** (~few µs) to avoid smearing when the beam jumps between distant endpoints, as opposed to raster scan's longer-persistence P22 phosphor.

### 1.4 Key Structural Components (Top-Level View)

1. **Display Controller / Display Processor (DP)** – interprets the display file and drives the hardware.
2. **Display File / Refresh Buffer** – a list of drawing commands (line, point, set color) with end-coordinates.
3. **Vector Generator (Character Generator / Line Generator)** – hardware that produces analog deflection signals.
4. **CRT with magnetic or electrostatic deflection yokes** – the actual beam-steering device.
5. **Keyboard / Input device** – used by the user to issue display commands in real-time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Random Scan Display Works (Step-by-Step)

A random scan system converts a stored list of geometric primitives into visible lines on a CRT. Here is the precise sequence of operations:

1. **Application Program Issues Commands**: The user's program (typically written in a graphics language like **PHIGS**, **GKS**, or older API) issues drawing commands such as `LINE(x1, y1, x2, y2)` or `POLYLINE`.

2. **Display File Construction**: The graphics software translates these commands into a **display file** stored in system memory. Each entry typically contains:
   - **Op-code** (e.g., DRAW, MOVE, SET COLOR)
   - **End-point coordinates** (x, y values)
   - **Intensity / color bits**

3. **Display Processor Polls the File**: The **Display Processor (DP)** repeatedly cycles through the display file at a fixed rate (the refresh rate, e.g., 60 Hz).

4. **Vector Generation**: For each `DRAW` command, the **Vector Generator (VG)** computes the analog X and Y deflection voltages needed to move the beam from the current endpoint to the new endpoint as a **straight line**.

5. **Beam Steering via Deflection Yokes**: The X and Y signals drive the **magnetic deflection yokes** around the CRT neck, steering the electron beam along the desired path.

6. **Z-Axis Modulation**: While the beam moves, the **Z-axis (intensity) is turned ON** to make the line visible; between lines, it is blanked.

7. **Refresh Loop**: After all commands are processed, the DP **wraps back** to the first entry and repeats — this continuous re-execution is what prevents flicker.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why use a Display Processor?** Because offloading the line-drawing from the main CPU allows **real-time interaction** (e.g., rotating a model) without redrawing it from scratch in software.
- **Why short-persistence phosphor?** Because if the beam jumps from one part of the screen to another, a long-persistence phosphor would leave a **trail** (ghosting). Short persistence keeps the picture crisp.
- **Why the magnetic (not electrostatic) deflection in most designs?** Magnetic deflection provides a **larger deflection angle** and flatter screen, suitable for the rapid, large-amplitude jumps random scan demands.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Time to draw one frame | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | $f_{refresh}$ = refresh rate | seconds |
| 2 | Time available per command | $T_{cmd} = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{N_{cmds}}$ | $N_{cmds}$ = total number of display commands | seconds |
| 3 | Vector drawing time | $t_v = \dfrac{L}{v_{beam}}$ | $L$ = line length, $v_{beam}$ = beam writing speed | seconds |
| 4 | Total drawing time per cycle | $T_{draw} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{v,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{b,j}$ | $N$ = lines, $M$ = blanks, $t_b$ = beam-blanking time | seconds |
| 5 | Flicker-free condition | $T_{draw} \le T_{frame}$ | — | — |
| 6 | Deflection bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{N_{cmds}}{T_{frame}}$ | — | commands/sec |
| 7 | Screen resolution | $R_x \times R_y$ | e.g., 1024 × 768 | pixels (logical) |
| 8 | Vector length on screen | $L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | endpoint coordinates | screen units |
| 9 | Refresh buffer size | $M = N \times W_{entry}$ | $W_{entry}$ = words per command | words |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board-Exam Insight:** The **flicker-free condition** $T_{draw} \le T_{frame}$ is the single most-asked formula in random scan topics. If the picture complexity grows so that $T_{draw} > T_{frame}$, the picture **flickers** because the DP cannot finish one pass before the next begins.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although pure random scan systems are now obsolete (replaced by raster CRTs and LCDs), the **architectural concept lives on** in:

- **Early CAD systems** (e.g., IBM 2250, IMLAC PDS-1, Tektronix 4014 terminals).
- **Oscilloscopes** (analog CROs use the exact random-scan vector principle to draw waveforms).
- **Pen plotters** (direct mechanical analog of a random scan system).
- **Modern GPU command buffers** (where drawing commands are stored as a *display list* and re-issued — a digital descendant of the random scan display file).
- **Aerospace HUDs** and early **flight simulators** (used random scan for crisp, flicker-free vector graphics at high refresh rates).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Flicker-Free Refresh Rate from Scene Complexity

**Problem Setup:** A random scan display contains a scene with $N$ straight-line vectors. Each vector takes an average drawing time of $t_v$ seconds, and each beam "flyback" (blanking) takes $t_b$ seconds. There are $N$ lines and $N$ inter-vector moves, so $N$ blanks.

**Step 1 — Total drawing time per cycle:**

$$
T_{draw} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{v,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N} t_{b,j}
$$

Since $t_{v,i}$ and $t_{b,j}$ are assumed uniform:

$$
T_{draw} = N \cdot t_v + N \cdot t_b = N \cdot (t_v + t_b)
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum time before flicker appears:**

For the picture to remain stable, the display processor must finish one pass within the frame interval:

$$
T_{draw} \le T_{frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for minimum refresh frequency:**

Substitute $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$:

$$
N \cdot (t_v + t_b) \le \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
f_{refresh} \le \dfrac{1}{N \cdot (t_v + t_b)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**

Given: $N = 1000$ vectors, $t_v = 1\ \mu s$, $t_b = 0.5\ \mu s$.

$$
T_{draw} = 1000 \cdot (1\ \mu s + 0.5\ \mu s) = 1000 \cdot 1.5\ \mu s = 1500\ \mu s = 1.5\ ms
$$

$$
f_{refresh,min} = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 666.7\ Hz
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

A minimum refresh rate of **666.7 Hz** is needed; any lower and the picture flickers. This illustrates why random scan systems are excellent for **moderately complex scenes** (a few hundred lines) — beyond a few thousand vectors, the required refresh rate becomes unrealistically high for the analog hardware of the era.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Vector Drawing Time from Screen Geometry

**Step 1 — Vector length on screen:**

For a line from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ in normalized screen coordinates $[-1, +1]$:

$$
L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}
$$

The factor-of-2 cancels in the ratio, so the unit can be the **screen diagonal fraction**.

**Step 2 — Drawing time:**

$$
t_v = \dfrac{L}{v_{beam}}
$$

where $v_{beam}$ is the linear writing speed of the beam across the phosphor, typically expressed in **screen units per microsecond**.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
t_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}{v_{beam}}
$$

**Step 4 — Example numeric check:**

Let $(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0)$ and $(x_2, y_2) = (0.6, 0.8)$ (a line covering 1.0 of the diagonal).

If $v_{beam} = 1\ \text{unit}/\mu s$:

$$
t_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.8^2}}{1} = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1.0\ \mu s
$$

The derivation is dimensionally and numerically consistent.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Random Scan vs. Raster Scan (Board-Favorite Comparison)

| Parameter | Random Scan (Vector) | Raster Scan (Bitmap) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Drawing primitive | Line-by-line (vectors) | Pixel-by-pixel (scan-lines) |
| Beam motion | Random order, jumps anywhere | Fixed left-to-right, top-to-bottom |
| Refresh memory contents | Display list (command-based) | Frame buffer (intensity per pixel) |
| Memory needed | **Very low** (kB range) | **High** (MB range) |
| Resolution | Practically infinite (analog) | Fixed (e.g., $1920 \times 1080$) |
| Refresh rate | Depends on scene complexity | Fixed by display standard (60/75 Hz) |
| Flicker | Yes, if scene too complex | No (independent of complexity) |
| Color | Hard (limited vector generators) | Easy (per-pixel color depth) |
| Fill capability | Poor (requires scan conversion) | Excellent (hardware fill) |
| Cost (historically) | Expensive DP, cheap memory | Cheap controller, expensive memory |
| Typical use | CAD, oscilloscopes, military | TV, monitors, phones |
| Phosphor | Short persistence (P31) | Long persistence (P22) |
| Deflection type | Magnetic (large angles) | Magnetic or electrostatic |

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Board-Style Numerical Problem

**Question:** A random scan display system has a refresh rate of $f = 50\ Hz$ and contains a scene with $N = 500$ vectors. The average time to draw one vector is $t_v = 2\ \mu s$ and the beam blanking time between vectors is $t_b = 1\ \mu s$.

**(a)** Find the total time taken by the display processor to draw one complete frame.

**(b)** Determine whether the picture will flicker.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Total drawing time per cycle:

$$
T_{draw} = N \cdot (t_v + t_b) = 500 \cdot (2\ \mu s + 1\ \mu s)
$$

$$
T_{draw} = 500 \cdot 3\ \mu s = 1500\ \mu s = 1.5\ ms
$$

**(b)** Compare with frame time:

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02\ s = 20\ ms
$$

Since:

$$
T_{draw} = 1.5\ ms \le 20\ ms = T_{frame}
$$

The picture **will NOT flicker** — the DP has ample margin ($\approx 18.5\ ms$ of idle time per frame).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Random Scan Display System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a classic random scan system, including data flow between the application, the display file, the display processor, the vector generator, and the CRT.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Program] -->|Graphics Commands LINE MOVE SET| B[Graphics Software Translator]
    B -->|Builds Display File| C[Display File Refresh Buffer]
    C -->|Sequential Read Cycle| D[Display Processor DP]
    D -->|Opcode Interpretation| E{Command Type}
    E -->|DRAW| F[Vector Generator X Y Voltages]
    E -->|MOVE| G[Beam Blanking Z OFF]
    F --> H[Deflection Yokes X and Y Coils]
    G --> H
    H --> I[CRT Electron Beam]
    F --> J[Z Axis Intensity Control]
    J --> I
    I -->|Phosphor Glow| K[Visible Picture on Screen]
    L[Keyboard Input] --> A
    D -->|Wraps back after last command| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Application Program** issues high-level graphics commands.
- The **Graphics Software Translator** converts them into a low-level display file.
- The **Display Processor** reads the file cyclically, and based on the op-code, either triggers the **Vector Generator** (to draw) or the **Blanking Circuit** (to lift the pen).
- The **X-Y Deflection Yokes** steer the beam; the **Z-axis** controls brightness.
- After processing the last command, the DP **wraps back to entry 1**, sustaining a continuous refresh loop.

---

### 4.2 State Diagram: Display Processor Execution Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitRead
    InitRead --> FetchOp: Read entry N
    FetchOp --> DecodeOp
    DecodeOp --> ExecDraw: Op = DRAW
    DecodeOp --> ExecMove: Op = MOVE
    DecodeOp --> ExecSet: Op = SET
    ExecDraw --> VectorGen: Compute X Y deflection
    ExecMove --> BeamBlank: Z axis OFF
    ExecSet --> UpdateState: Update color or line style
    VectorGen --> WaitNext: Line drawn
    BeamBlank --> WaitNext: Beam repositioned
    UpdateState --> WaitNext: State updated
    WaitNext --> IncrPtr: Increment pointer
    IncrPtr --> FetchOp: If N less than Nmax
    IncrPtr --> WrapPtr: If N equals Nmax
    WrapPtr --> FetchOp: Pointer reset to 1
```

This state machine captures the **inner loop** of the display processor. It is precisely this loop that must complete within $T_{frame}$ to avoid flicker.

---

### 4.3 Data Structure of a Typical Display File Entry

A single entry in the display file typically contains the following fields (illustrated as a horizontal layout):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Opcode Field 4 bits] --> B[Color Field 4 bits]
    B --> C[Intensity Field 4 bits]
    C --> D[Line Style 2 bits]
    D --> E[X1 Coordinate 12 bits]
    E --> F[Y1 Coordinate 12 bits]
    F --> G[X2 Coordinate 12 bits]
    G --> H[Y2 Coordinate 12 bits]
```

**Total entry size = 4 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 12 + 12 + 12 + 12 = 62 bits ≈ 8 bytes** per vector, which is why random scan systems are extremely memory-efficient.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define a Random Scan Display System. List any two advantages of random scan over raster scan.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

A **Random Scan Display System** is a CRT-based display device in which the electron beam is steered directly along the lines of the picture, in any order, using a stored **display file** of line-drawing commands, and the picture is sustained by repeatedly re-executing the display file at a fixed refresh rate.

**Two advantages over raster scan:**
1. **Higher resolution** — since lines are drawn as continuous analog vectors, there is no pixelation; resolution is limited only by the CRT spot size and deflection accuracy.
2. **Lower memory requirement** — the display stores only vector endpoints (typically 8 bytes/line) instead of every pixel in a frame buffer; this is **orders of magnitude less memory**.

*(Mention any two from: smooth lines, smooth animation, no aliasing, cost-effective for line drawings, easy real-time rotation/scaling of objects.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is a Display File? Why is it necessary in a random scan system?**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

A **Display File** (also called a **Refresh Buffer** or **Display List**) is a structured region of memory containing a list of graphics commands — each entry consisting of an **op-code** (e.g., DRAW, MOVE, SET COLOR) and **end-point coordinates** of the line to be drawn.

**Necessity:**
The CRT phosphor has **very short persistence** (a few µs), so the picture fades almost instantly once the beam moves away. To keep the image visible, the same picture must be **re-drawn 30 to 60 times per second**. The display file is the **stored representation** that the Display Processor reads **continuously and cyclically** to refresh the picture. Without it, the picture would vanish in a fraction of a second.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Random Scan Display System** with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the **Display Processor**, **Display File**, and **Vector Generator**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A random scan system has a refresh rate of **40 Hz** and contains a scene with **800 vectors**. Average drawing time per vector is **2.5 µs** and blanking time between vectors is **1 µs**.
    **(i)** Find the total time taken to draw one complete frame.
    **(ii)** Will the picture flicker? Justify with calculation.
    **(iii)** If the scene complexity increases to 3000 vectors (same $t_v$ and $t_b$), what is the **minimum** refresh rate required to avoid flicker? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Architecture and Component Functions (7 marks):**

The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The major blocks and their functions are:

1. **Application Program & Graphics Software:** Accept user commands (LINE, MOVE, POLYGON) and translate them into a list of low-level display commands stored in the display file.

2. **Display File / Refresh Buffer:** A memory-resident list of drawing instructions. Each entry contains:
   - Op-code (DRAW / MOVE / SET),
   - End-point coordinates $(x, y)$,
   - Optional: color, intensity, line-style bits.

3. **Display Processor (DP):** The "conductor" of the system. Its functions are:
   - **Cyclicly reads** the display file from beginning to end.
   - **Interprets** the op-code.
   - **Sends** the endpoint coordinates to the Vector Generator for DRAW commands.
   - **Sends** blanking signals for MOVE commands.
   - **Wraps back** to entry 1 after the last command.
   - Operates independently of the main CPU, allowing **real-time animation**.

4. **Vector Generator (VG):** A hardware unit (analog multiplier + DAC) that takes two digital endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ and produces a **time-varying analog X and Y deflection voltage** that linearly interpolates the beam position between the two endpoints — effectively drawing a perfectly straight line at the speed determined by the system clock.

5. **Deflection Yokes & Z-axis:** Magnetic coils steer the electron beam; the Z-axis grid controls intensity (ON during DRAW, OFF during MOVE).

6. **CRT with Short-Persistence Phosphor:** Displays the line; the short persistence prevents smearing during beam jumps.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total drawing time for 800 vectors:**

$$
T_{draw} = N \cdot (t_v + t_b) = 800 \cdot (2.5\ \mu s + 1\ \mu s)
$$

$$
T_{draw} = 800 \cdot 3.5\ \mu s = 2800\ \mu s = 2.8\ ms
$$

**(ii) Will it flicker?**

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{40} = 0.025\ s = 25\ ms
$$

Since $T_{draw} = 2.8\ ms \le 25\ ms = T_{frame}$, the picture **will NOT flicker** (huge margin of $22.2\ ms$).

**(iii) Minimum refresh rate for 3000 vectors:**

$$
T_{draw,new} = 3000 \cdot 3.5\ \mu s = 10{,}500\ \mu s = 10.5\ ms
$$

For flicker-free operation:

$$
T_{draw,new} \le T_{frame,min} = \dfrac{1}{f_{min}}
$$

$$
f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{T_{draw,new}} = \dfrac{1}{10.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 95.24\ Hz
$$

**Result: $f_{min} \approx 95.24\ Hz$** — note that this is well above the standard 60 Hz, demonstrating the scalability problem of random scan systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Random Scan** and **Raster Scan** display systems based on **eight key parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **working of a vector (random scan) display system** with the help of the display processor's cyclic operation. Show how a line segment from $(10, 20)$ to $(60, 90)$ is processed in the display file, and compute the **time required to draw this vector** if the beam writing speed is $0.5\ \text{units}/\mu s$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Random Scan vs. Raster Scan Comparison (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3**. Eight key comparison points are:

1. **Drawing technique** (line-by-line vs. pixel-by-pixel)
2. **Refresh memory type** (display list vs. frame buffer)
3. **Memory requirement** (kB vs. MB)
4. **Resolution** (analog infinite vs. fixed pixel grid)
5. **Refresh dependency** (complexity-dependent vs. fixed 60 Hz)
6. **Flicker behavior** (flickers if too complex vs. never flickers due to complexity)
7. **Color/area fill capability** (poor vs. excellent)
8. **Cost structure** (expensive DP, cheap memory vs. cheap controller, expensive memory)

*(For full marks, write 2–3 lines for each parameter, not just the heading.)*

---

**(b) Working of a Vector Display and Drawing Time Calculation (7 marks):**

**Working of the vector display system:**

The display processor **continuously and cyclically** reads the display file. For each entry:
- If **op-code = DRAW**, it sends the $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ coordinates to the **Vector Generator**, which produces linear analog deflection voltages, while the **Z-axis** is turned ON, drawing a glowing line on the phosphor.
- If **op-code = MOVE**, the Z-axis is **blanked** (beam OFF) and the deflection system repositions the beam to the new start point.
- After the final entry, the DP **wraps the pointer** back to the first entry and begins the next refresh cycle.

**Display file entry for line from $(10, 20)$ to $(60, 90)$:**

A typical binary representation of the entry might look like:

```
| Opcode (4b) | Color (4b) | Intensity (4b) | Style (2b) | x1=10 (12b) | y1=20 (12b) | x2=60 (12b) | y2=90 (12b) |
|   0001      |   0010     |    1111        |    00      |  000000001010 | 000000010100 | 000000111100 | 000001011010 |
```

**Time required to draw the vector:**

Vector length:

$$
L = \sqrt{(60 - 10)^2 + (90 - 20)^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 70^2}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{2500 + 4900} = \sqrt{7400} \approx 86.02\ \text{units}
$$

Drawing time:

$$
t_v = \dfrac{L}{v_{beam}} = \dfrac{86.02}{0.5\ \text{units}/\mu s} \approx 172.05\ \mu s
$$

**Result: $t_v \approx 172\ \mu s$ per vector.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are deducted:**
> 1. **Confusing "Random" with "random image"** — students often write that "random scan displays random images." This is wrong. *Random* refers to the **non-sequential beam path**, not the content. Heavy deduction (up to 1 mark).
> 2. **Forgetting the flicker condition** $T_{draw} \le T_{frame}$ — in numerical problems, students often compute $T_{draw}$ correctly but **omit the comparison step** that decides flicker. Always write the conclusion explicitly: *"Since $T_{draw} \le T_{frame}$, the picture does NOT flicker."*
> 3. **Wrong units** — $T_{draw}$ comes out in $\mu s$ but $T_{frame}$ is in $s$ or $ms$. Convert before comparing, or write both in the **same unit**. Examiners specifically look for this conversion.
> 4. **Omitting the Display Processor from the block diagram** — even if you remember the display file, skipping the DP loses you 2 marks in 7-mark architecture questions.
> 5. **Mixing up MOVE and DRAW op-codes** — MOVE blanks the beam; DRAW keeps it ON. Swapping these in an explanation is a common but costly error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Random Scan Display** = vector/stroke/calligraphic display — draws **line by line** in any order.
- **Core components:** Application → Graphics Software → **Display File** → **Display Processor** → **Vector Generator** → **Deflection Yokes** → **CRT**.
- **Display File** stores op-codes + endpoints; cyclically read by the DP.
- **Display Processor (DP)** is the heart of the system — runs the refresh loop.
- **Vector Generator** produces analog X, Y deflection voltages for straight lines.
- **Z-axis** controls intensity: ON for DRAW, OFF for MOVE.
- **Phosphor** is short-persistence (P31) to avoid smearing during beam jumps.
- **Flicker condition:** $T_{draw} \le T_{frame}$, where $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$.
- **Vector drawing time:** $t_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}{v_{beam}}$.
- **Refresh rate** typically **30–60 Hz** in classic systems; must be **higher** for complex scenes.
- **Memory** per vector ≈ **8 bytes** (very low) vs. raster scan's MB-level frame buffer.
- **Resolution** is **analog / virtually infinite** (no pixel grid).
- **Advantages:** sharp lines, low memory, real-time vector animation, excellent for line drawings.
- **Disadvantages:** no area fill, color hard, flickers with complex scenes, expensive analog hardware.
- **Random ≠ random image** — *Random* = non-sequential beam path.
- **Modern descendants:** oscilloscopes, pen plotters, GPU command buffers/display lists.
- **Key formula to memorize for KTU exams:** $T_{draw} = N \cdot (t_v + t_b)$ and $f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot (t_v + t_b)}$.
- **Comparison must include 8 parameters** (drawing technique, memory type, memory size, resolution, refresh dependency, flicker, fill capability, cost).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raster Scan Displays and Systems. Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT602 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Raster Scan Displays and Systems. Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Raster Scan Display — Formal Academic Definition

A **Raster Scan Display** is a cathode-ray-tube (CRT) or flat-panel (LCD/LED) display technology in which the screen is treated as a two-dimensional matrix of discrete picture elements (pixels), and the image is produced by systematically illuminating these pixels one row (scan-line) at a time, sweeping from the top-left of the screen to the bottom-right, and then retracing back to the top — repeatedly at a fixed **refresh rate** (commonly **60 Hz** or **75 Hz**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** In a raster system, each screen position (x, y) is mapped to a memory location called a *frame buffer*. The intensity/color of every pixel is stored as a binary code in this buffer, and a *video controller* reads it sequentially to paint the picture. This is the de-facto standard for nearly all modern monitors, TVs, and embedded LCD panels.

The core functional components that govern the system are:
- **Resolution** = total number of pixels in the grid (e.g., **1920 × 1080**, **1366 × 768**).
- **Aspect Ratio** = width-to-height ratio (e.g., **16:9** for HD displays).
- **Refresh Rate (f)** = number of complete screen redraws per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Frame Buffer (Video RAM)** = dedicated memory holding one intensity value per pixel.
- **Color Depth / Bit-Plane Depth (n)** = number of bits used per pixel (e.g., **24 bits = 16.7 million colors**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Giant Lite-Brite"

Imagine a **Lite-Brite toy** — a black board punched with thousands of tiny holes in a perfect rectangular grid. To "draw" a picture, you insert colored pegs into specific holes. Your eyes see a coherent image, but the computer actually only knows which **specific hole (pixel)** to light up.

The raster system works the same way, but with two important differences:
1. The board is **rewritten 60 times every second** by a sweeping electron beam (or a TFT scanning circuit in LCDs), so the picture appears stable and flicker-free.
2. Instead of physical pegs, each pixel position stores a number (0–255) in a memory cell — the **frame buffer** — which the video controller consults to decide "how bright" or "what color" each peg should glow.

The frame buffer is the **single source of truth** for what you see on the screen. The CPU/host never draws pixels directly — it merely **updates the frame buffer**, and the display hardware continuously translates the buffer into a visible image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Raster"?** The word *raster* comes from the Latin *rastrum* (a rake). Just as a rake drags across soil in evenly spaced parallel strokes, the electron beam drags across the phosphor screen in evenly spaced parallel scan lines — producing the visible image.

## 1.3 Line Drawing Problem — The Motivation for DDA

A fundamental primitive in any graphics system is the ability to draw a straight line between two user-defined endpoints $\mathbf{P_1 = (x_1, y_1)}$ and $\mathbf{P_2 = (x_2, y_2)}$. In a continuous mathematical plane, this is trivial:

$$y = mx + c \quad \text{where} \quad m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

However, in a **discrete pixel grid**, we cannot "draw a continuous line" — we can only choose a finite set of integer pixel coordinates. The challenge is to select a *visually convincing* set of pixels that approximates the mathematical line as closely as possible. The **Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA)** algorithm is the simplest, most direct way to do this by incrementally stepping along the line equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Raster Scan Display System

A complete raster graphics system is a tightly coupled pipeline of five functional blocks. Each block has a specialized role, and KTU frequently asks students to enumerate and describe these blocks.

| # | Block | Function |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Host CPU** | Runs the application, issues high-level graphics commands (e.g., `drawLine(x1, y1, x2, y2)`). |
| 2 | **Display Processor (Graphics Controller)** | Interprets the high-level commands, performs scan conversion, clipping, transformation, and writes pixel values into the frame buffer. |
| 3 | **Frame Buffer (Video RAM)** | A dual-ported memory that stores one intensity/color code per pixel. For a $1920 \times 1080$, 24-bit display, it requires $\approx 6.2$ MB. |
| 4 | **Video Controller (CRTC)** | Reads the frame buffer row-by-row, applies color-lookup (for indexed-color modes), and produces the analog (VGA) or digital (HDMI/DisplayPort) video signal. |
| 5 | **Display Device (Monitor / LCD Panel)** | Physically converts the video signal into visible light through a CRT phosphor or TFT liquid-crystal array. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interlaced vs. Progressive Scan:** In *interlaced* mode, the display refreshes all **odd** rows in one pass (the "odd field") and all **even** rows in the next (the "even field"), giving a perceived 50/60 Hz refresh at half the actual bandwidth. In *progressive* (non-interlaced) mode, every row is redrawn on every pass — this is the modern standard (e.g., 1080p).

## 2.2 Frame Buffer Capacity — The Core Memory Equation

The size of the frame buffer is dictated by the resolution and color depth:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{size}} = R_x \times R_y \times n \quad \text{(bits)}}$$

where $R_x \times R_y$ is the screen resolution and $n$ is the number of bits per pixel.

The number of **distinct colors** displayable is:

$$\boxed{N_{\text{colors}} = 2^n}$$

The **bandwidth** required to refresh the display once per frame is:

$$\boxed{B = R_x \times R_y \times n \times f \quad \text{(bits/sec)}}$$

where $f$ is the refresh frequency.

## 2.3 The DDA Algorithm — Theoretical Foundation

The DDA algorithm is derived from the **differential form** of the straight-line equation. Starting from $y = mx + c$, taking the derivative gives:

$$dy = m \cdot dx$$

This means a *unit* change in $x$ ($dx = 1$) produces a change of $m$ in $y$. Conversely, a *unit* change in $y$ ($dy = 1$) produces a change of $1/m$ in $x$. The DDA algorithm uses this insight to **incrementally step** along the line, choosing the major axis (x or y) based on the slope.

### 2.3.1 Case Analysis on the Slope

| Slope Condition | Major Axis | Step Action | Why |
|-----------------|------------|-------------|-----|
| $\vert m \vert \leq 1$ | x-axis | $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$; $y_{k+1} = y_k + m$ | x changes faster, so we sample one pixel per x-step. |
| $\vert m \vert \geq 1$ | y-axis | $y_{k+1} = y_k + 1$; $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1/m$ | y changes faster, so we sample one pixel per y-step. |

### 2.3.2 Number of Plotting Steps

To ensure the line is traced exactly from $\mathbf{P_1}$ to $\mathbf{P_2}$, the number of plotting steps equals the largest projected displacement:

$$\boxed{\text{Steps} = \max\bigl(\,\vert \Delta x \vert,\; \vert \Delta y \vert\,\bigr)}$$

The fractional increments are:

$$\boxed{\Delta x_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta x}{\text{Steps}}, \qquad \Delta y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\text{Steps}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Use:** DDA is the conceptual ancestor of every modern hardware line-drawing primitive in GPUs. Even modern Bresenham's algorithm, used in silicon, is mathematically equivalent — it just avoids floating-point arithmetic by replacing multiplication with bit-shifts.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---------|------------|----------------|
| Slope of line | $m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | Direction and steepness of the line. |
| Line equation (slope-intercept) | $y = mx + c$ | Continuous mathematical form. |
| Line equation (two-point form) | $\dfrac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | Symmetric form, no division by zero. |
| Differential form | $dy = m \cdot dx$ | Foundation of DDA. |
| DDA step count | $\text{Steps} = \max\bigl(\,\vert \Delta x \vert,\; \vert \Delta y \vert\,\bigr)$ | Avoids early termination. |
| Frame-buffer size | $F = R_x \cdot R_y \cdot n$ | Total VRAM needed. |
| Display bandwidth | $B = R_x \cdot R_y \cdot n \cdot f$ | Data rate (bits/sec) for refresh. |
| Number of colors | $N = 2^n$ | For an $n$-bit color depth. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The frame-buffer equation is directly used by SoC designers when sizing the integrated video RAM in mobile GPUs (e.g., Mali, Adreno). The bandwidth equation determines the choice of memory interface (DDR, LPDDR) and bus width (32-bit, 64-bit).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DDA Algorithm

We begin with the **continuous slope-intercept** equation of a straight line:

$$y = m \cdot x + c$$

where the slope $m$ and the y-intercept $c$ are given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \\
c &= y_1 - m \cdot x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the **differential** of both sides with respect to $x$, we obtain the rate of change of $y$ per unit step in $x$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = m \quad \Rightarrow \quad dy = m \cdot dx$$

**Interpretation:** A unit increment in $x$ ($dx = 1$) produces a corresponding increment in $y$ equal to $m$. This is the *core insight* on which DDA rests — we don't need to recompute the line equation at every pixel; we just *accumulate* the slope.

For a steep line where $\vert m \vert > 1$, it is more efficient to invert the relation and step in $y$:

$$\frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{1}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dx = \frac{dy}{m}$$

A unit increment in $y$ ($dy = 1$) then produces an increment in $x$ of $1/m$.

### 3.1.1 Generalised Discrete Recurrence

For the $k$-th plotting step, the next pixel coordinates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + \Delta x_{\text{inc}} \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + \Delta y_{\text{inc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the per-step increments are chosen so that after exactly $N = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ steps, we land precisely on $(x_2, y_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_{\text{inc}} &= \frac{\Delta x}{N} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{N} \\
\Delta y_{\text{inc}} &= \frac{\Delta y}{N} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial conditions:** $x_0 = x_1$, $y_0 = y_1$.

**Termination condition:** after $N$ iterations, the algorithm must produce $x_N = x_2$ and $y_N = y_2$ (within rounding tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Round-off Problem:** Because $\Delta x_{\text{inc}}$ and $\Delta y_{\text{inc}}$ are typically floating-point (non-integer) values, DDA must perform a `round()` (or `floor(x + 0.5)`) operation at every step to convert the floating-point coordinate back to a valid integer pixel. This rounding accumulates, and over long lines (or low-precision hardware) it produces visible *staircase drift* — this is DDA's primary weakness, and the reason Bresenham's algorithm was invented.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — DDA Line from $(2, 3)$ to $(8, 11)$

We now trace the DDA algorithm **step-by-step** for the endpoints $P_1 = (2, 3)$ and $P_2 = (8, 11)$.

### Step 1 — Compute Displacements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= x_2 - x_1 = 8 - 2 = 6 \\
\Delta y &= y_2 - y_1 = 11 - 3 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Slope

$$m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$$

Since $\vert m \vert = 1.333 > 1$, the line is **steep**, and we will step along the y-axis (one pixel per y-step).

### Step 3 — Determine the Number of Steps

$$\text{Steps} = \max\bigl(\,\vert \Delta x \vert,\; \vert \Delta y \vert\,\bigr) = \max(6,\; 8) = 8$$

### Step 4 — Compute Per-Step Increments

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_{\text{inc}} &= \frac{\Delta x}{\text{Steps}} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75 \\
\Delta y_{\text{inc}} &= \frac{\Delta y}{\text{Steps}} = \frac{8}{8} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Iterate the Recurrence

Initialize $x_0 = 2.00$ and $y_0 = 3.00$, then apply the recurrence for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 7$:

| Step $k$ | $x_k$ (float) | $y_k$ (float) | $\text{round}(x_k)$ | $\text{round}(y_k)$ | Plotted Pixel |
|----------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 0 | 2.00 | 3.00 | 2 | 3 | **(2, 3)** |
| 1 | 2.75 | 4.00 | 3 | 4 | **(3, 4)** |
| 2 | 3.50 | 5.00 | 4 | 5 | **(4, 5)** |
| 3 | 4.25 | 6.00 | 4 | 6 | **(4, 6)** |
| 4 | 5.00 | 7.00 | 5 | 7 | **(5, 7)** |
| 5 | 5.75 | 8.00 | 6 | 8 | **(6, 8)** |
| 6 | 6.50 | 9.00 | 7 | 9 | **(7, 9)** |
| 7 | 7.25 | 10.00 | 7 | 10 | **(7, 10)** |
| 8 | 8.00 | 11.00 | 8 | 11 | **(8, 11)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Termination:** At step 8, we have $x_8 = 8.00$ and $y_8 = 11.00$, which exactly matches the target endpoint $P_2 = (8, 11)$. The algorithm has therefore correctly produced a sequence of **9 pixels** (steps 0 through 8 inclusive) that visually approximates the line.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison — DDA vs. Theoretical

| Metric | Theoretical (Continuous) | DDA Result |
|--------|---------------------------|------------|
| Line length | $\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = 10$ units | 9 pixels (closest integer samples) |
| Endpoint reached? | Exactly at $P_2$ | Yes — $(8, 11)$ matches |
| Slope error | 0 | $\leq 0.5$ pixel per step (rounding) |
| Floating-point ops per step | 2 adds | 2 adds + 2 rounds (or truncations) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drift Observation:** Notice that at step 3 the $x$-coordinate is $4.25$, which rounds to **4** — the same as step 2. The line therefore has two consecutive plotted pixels with the same $x$-value (4, 5) and (4, 6). This is a *legitimate* behavior for DDA on steep lines, and it visually creates a slightly thicker rendering than a true continuous line. The accumulated drift over the 8 steps is $\leq 0.5$ pixel, which is the best DDA can guarantee.

## 3.4 Closed-Form Termination Proof

To prove that the algorithm reaches $P_2$ exactly, observe that after $N$ iterations, the accumulated increments must sum to the original displacement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_N &= x_0 + N \cdot \Delta x_{\text{inc}} = x_1 + N \cdot \frac{x_2 - x_1}{N} = x_1 + (x_2 - x_1) = x_2 \\
y_N &= y_0 + N \cdot \Delta y_{\text{inc}} = y_1 + N \cdot \frac{y_2 - y_1}{N} = y_1 + (y_2 - y_1) = y_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This algebraic identity proves **exact endpoint convergence** in the floating-point domain, even though individual plotted pixels may be off by $\pm 0.5$ due to rounding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Raster Scan Display System — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete data flow from the application layer down to the physical display panel. Each block represents a distinct functional unit, and the arrows denote the direction of data movement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Host CPU Application Layer]
    A2[Graphics Command Buffer]
    A3[Display Processor Scan Conversion]
    A4[Frame Buffer Video RAM]
    A5[Video Controller CRTC]
    A6[Monitor LCD or CRT Panel]
    A1 -- Issues Commands --> A2
    A2 -- High Level Primitives --> A3
    A3 -- Pixel Intensity Codes --> A4
    A4 -- Row by Row Readout --> A5
    A5 -- Analog VGA or Digital HDMI Signal --> A6
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The Host CPU **never** directly addresses pixels. It issues abstract commands like "draw line from A to B" or "fill rectangle with red."
- The **Display Processor** (also called a graphics accelerator) is the workhorse: it performs scan conversion (DDA, Bresenham), clipping, windowing, and 2D transformations.
- The **Frame Buffer** is dual-ported so the display processor can *write* to it while the video controller *reads* from it simultaneously — preventing visible tearing.
- The **Video Controller** generates the synchronization pulses (HSYNC, VSYNC) and continuously cycles through the frame buffer at the refresh rate.

## 4.2 DDA Algorithm — Control-Flow Flowchart

The flowchart below captures the iterative logic of the DDA algorithm in a form suitable for KTU exam diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start]
    B2[Read Endpoints x1, y1, x2, y2]
    B3[Compute dx = x2 minus x1 and dy = y2 minus y1]
    B4[Compute Steps = max absolute dx absolute dy]
    B5[Compute xinc = dx divided by Steps]
    B6[Compute yinc = dy divided by Steps]
    B7[Initialize x = x1 and y = y1]
    B8[Plot pixel at round x and round y]
    B9[Update x = x plus xinc and y = y plus yinc]
    B10{Is k equal to Steps?}
    B11[End]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8 --> B9 --> B10
    B10 -- No --> B8
    B10 -- Yes --> B11
```

**Decision points to remember:**

- The **loop guard** is the step counter, *not* a coordinate comparison — this is what guarantees endpoint convergence.
- The **`round()`** operation is the only non-trivial pixel-mapping function; in hardware it is implemented as a `floor(x + 0.5)` operation.
- The algorithm assumes $x_1 \neq x_2$ **or** $y_1 \neq y_2$; a "line" of length zero (a single point) is a degenerate case that should be guarded against in practice.

## 4.3 Pixel-Grid Visualization of the Worked Example

The following Mermaid **graph** diagram emulates a 10×10 pixel coordinate grid. Each node labeled `P_K_XY` represents the $k$-th pixel plotted by the DDA algorithm for the line from $(2, 3)$ to $(8, 11)$. The arrows show the plotting order.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Step 0: P at 2,3]
    P1[Step 1: P at 3,4]
    P2[Step 2: P at 4,5]
    P3[Step 3: P at 4,6]
    P4[Step 4: P at 5,7]
    P5[Step 5: P at 6,8]
    P6[Step 6: P at 7,9]
    P7[Step 7: P at 7,10]
    P8[Step 8: P at 8,11]
    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Pattern:** Observe that the y-coordinate advances by exactly 1 at every step (since $\vert m \vert > 1$), while the x-coordinate advances by ~0.75 per step. The integer rounding occasionally repeats an x-value (e.g., at steps 2 and 3, both $x = 4$), which creates a visually *thicker* vertical segment — this is the signature appearance of DDA on steep lines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

### Question 1. (3 Marks)
**What is a raster scan display? How does it differ from a random scan (vector) display in terms of image refresh mechanism?**

**Model Answer:**
A raster scan display treats the screen as a matrix of discrete pixels and refreshes the entire screen uniformly from top to bottom in sequential scan lines, regardless of the image complexity. The image is stored in a *frame buffer*, and the video controller reads it row-by-row at the refresh rate.

A random (vector) scan display, in contrast, directly draws the image as a set of line segments using a beam that moves from one endpoint to another. It refreshes *only* the line segments on the screen, not the entire grid, which can lead to flicker for complex images. Raster scan is well suited to realistic, shaded, and color images; random scan is well suited to engineering (CAD) wireframe drawings. **(3 marks — for clear distinction + frame-buffer reference)**

### Question 2. (3 Marks)
**Define the Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA) algorithm. Why is it called a "differential" algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**
The DDA algorithm is an incremental method for scan-converting a straight line into a set of pixel positions. It uses the *differential* form of the line equation ($dy = m \cdot dx$ or $dx = dy/m$) to step from one pixel to the next by a fixed fractional increment along the major axis.

It is called a "differential" algorithm because its recurrence relation is derived directly from the **differential** (rate-of-change) form of the line equation, rather than from the integral (slope-intercept) form. This makes the algorithm O(N) in the number of plotted pixels and avoids re-evaluating the line equation at every step. **(3 marks — for definition + differential reference + O(N) mention)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern, Module 1, Q1 of Question Paper]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Choice 1*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a **raster scan display system** with the help of a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. What is the role of the frame buffer and how is its size determined?

**Model Solution:**
