[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **What is a 2D rotation transformation? Write the rotation matrix used to rotate a point by an angle θ counter-clockwise about the origin.** **Model Answer:** > A 2D rotation transformation repositions a point $(x, y)$ by revolving it through an angle θ about a pivot point, while keeping the distance from the pivot constant. The standard CCW rotation matrix about the origin is: > > $$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ > > Applying this matrix to a point gives the new coordinates: > > $$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$ > > $$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$ > > Rotation is a **rigid-body transformation** that preserves shape, size, and distance. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between CCW and CW rotation. What is the relationship between the two rotation matrices?** **Model Answer:** > In the KTU / right-handed coordinate convention, **counter-clockwise (CCW)** rotation is taken as the **positive** direction, while **clockwise (CW)** rotation is **negative**. The two rotation matrices are: > > $$R_{CCW}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ > > $$R_{CW}(\theta) = R_{CCW}(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ > > **Relationship:** The CW matrix is obtained by negating θ in the CCW matrix (or equivalently, by **transposing** the CCW matrix, since the rotation matrix is orthogonal). The off-diagonal signs are flipped while the diagonal entries remain the same. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Pivot Rotation** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A triangle has vertices **A(2, 3)**, **B(6, 4)**, and **C(4, 7)**. Perform a **counter-clockwise rotation by 60°** about the **pivot point P(2, 1)**. Find the new coordinates of the triangle's vertices using the composite formula. **(b) [7 Marks]** Construct the **3×3 homogeneous transformation matrix** for the above rotation, and verify that multiplying it with one of the original vertices (say A) gives the same result as the composite formula. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) Solution:** **Given:** - Vertices: $A(2, 3), B(6, 4), C(4, 7)$ - Pivot: $P(x_r, y_r) = (2, 1)$ - Angle: $\theta = 60°$ **Step 1: Convert angle to radians and identify trig values.** $$\theta = 60° = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$$ $$\cos 60° = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$ **Step 2: State the composite rotation formulas (about arbitrary pivot).** $$x' = x_r + (x - x_r)\cos\theta - (y - y_r)\sin\theta$$ $$y' = y_r + (x - x_r)\sin\theta + (y - y_r)\cos\theta$$ **Step 3: Apply to vertex A(2, 3).** $$x_A' = 2 + (2 - 2)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - (3 - 1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 2 + 0 - \sqrt{3} = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.268$$ $$y_A' = 1 + (2 - 2)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + (3 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2$$ $$\boxed{A' = (2 - \sqrt{3},\ 2) \approx (0.268, 2)}$$ **Step 4: Apply to vertex B(6, 4).** $$x_B' = 2 + (6 - 2)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - (4 - 1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 2 + 2 - \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} = 4 - \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 1.402$$ $$y_B' = 1 + (6 - 2)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + (4 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 + 2\sqrt{3} + 1.5 \approx 5.964$$ $$\boxed{B' = \left(4 - \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2},\ 2.5 + 2\sqrt{3}\right) \approx (1.402, 5.964)}$$ **Step 5: Apply to vertex C(4, 7).** $$x_C' = 2 + (4 - 2)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - (7 - 1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 2 + 1 - 3\sqrt{3} = 3 - 3\sqrt{3} \approx -2.196$$ $$y_C' = 1 + (4 - 2)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + (7 - 1)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 + \sqrt{3} + 3 = 4 + \sqrt{3} \approx 5.732$$ $$\boxed{C' = (3 - 3\sqrt{3},\ 4 + \sqrt{3}) \approx (-2.196, 5.732)}$$ #### **Part (b) Solution — Homogeneous Matrix Construction and Verification:** **Step 1: Build the three constituent 3×3 matrices.** $$T(-2, -1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R(60°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T(2, 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Compute the composite $T(2,1) \cdot R(60°) \cdot T(-2,-1)$ using the closed-form formula.** Translation column entries: $$t_x = x_r(1 - \cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta = 2(1 - 0.5) + 1(0.866) = 1 + 0.866 = 1.866$$ $$t_y = -x_r\sin\theta + y_r(1 - \cos\theta) = -2(0.866) + 1(0.5) = -1.732 + 0.5 = -1.232$$ **Step 3: The full 3×3 composite matrix.** $$R_{pivot} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 1.866 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & -1.232 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4: Verify by applying to vertex A(2, 3) as homogeneous vector $(2, 3, 1)^T$.** $$\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 1.866 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & -1.232 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$x' = 0.5(2) + (-0.866)(3) + 1.866(1) = 1.0 - 2.598 + 1.866 = 0.268 \approx 2 - \sqrt{3} \checkmark$$ $$y' = 0.866(2) + 0.5(3) + (-1.232)(1) = 1.732 + 1.5 - 1.232 = 2.0 \checkmark$$ $$\therefore A' = (0.268, 2.0) \text{ in decimals} = (2 - \sqrt{3}, 2) \text{ in exact form}$$ The result matches part (a). ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Rotation About Origin with Geometric Sketching** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Given a square with vertices **P(1, 1)**, **Q(4, 1)**, **R(4, 4)**, and **S(1, 4)**, perform a **clockwise rotation by 45° about the origin**. Find the new positions of all four vertices. **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the same square, now perform a **counter-clockwise rotation by 90° about the pivot T(2, 2)** (which is the centroid of the square). Use the **3-step composite method** (translate, rotate, translate-back) and present the 3×3 homogeneous matrix for the operation. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) Solution:** **Given:** - Square vertices: $P(1, 1), Q(4, 1), R(4, 4), S(1, 4)$ - Direction: **Clockwise** by 45° - Pivot: Origin (0, 0) **Step 1: Use the CW rotation matrix (θ = −45°).** $$R_{CW}(45°) = R(-45°) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45° & \sin 45° \\ -\sin 45° & \cos 45° \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Apply to each vertex.** **For P(1, 1):** $$x_P' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$ $$y_P' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1) = 0$$ $$P' = (\sqrt{2},\ 0)$$ **For Q(4, 1):** $$x_Q' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 + 1) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 3.536$$ $$y_Q' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-4 + 1) = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -2.121$$ $$Q' = \left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}},\ \frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx (3.536, -2.121)$$ **For R(4, 4):** $$x_R' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 + 4) = \frac{8}{\sqrt{2}} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657$$ $$y_R' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-4 + 4) = 0$$ $$R' = (4\sqrt{2},\ 0) \approx (5.657, 0)$$ **For S(1, 4):** $$x_S' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + 4) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 3.536$$ $$y_S' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1 + 4) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 2.121$$ $$S' = \left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}},\ \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx (3.536, 2.121)$$ **Step 3: Note the geometric pattern.** Because the original square was axis-aligned and CW rotation by 45° maps the corners to the **diagonal axis**, points P and R both end up on the x-axis (since they were originally on the diagonal y = x). This is an important sanity check. #### **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1: Constituent matrices with θ = 90° (CCW), pivot = (2, 2).** $$T(-2, -2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T(2, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Apply the 3-step method manually to vertex P(1, 1).** **Sub-step 2.1 — Translate to origin:** $$P_1 = T(-2, -2) \cdot (1, 1, 1)^T = (1 - 2,\ 1 - 2,\ 1)^T = (-1, -1, 1)^T$$ **Sub-step 2.2 — Rotate:** $$P_2 = R(90°) \cdot (-1, -1, 1)^T = (0 \cdot (-1) + (-1)(-1) + 0,\ 1 \cdot (-1) + 0 \cdot (-1) + 0,\ 1)^T = (1, -1, 1)^T$$ **Sub-step 2.3 — Translate back:** $$P' = T(2, 2) \cdot (1, -1, 1)^T = (1 + 2, -1 + 2, 1)^T = (3, 1, 1)^T$$ **Result: $P' = (3, 1)$.** **Step 3: The full composite 3×3 matrix using the closed-form formula with $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$, $x_r = y_r = 2$:** $$t_x = 2(1 - 0) + 2(1) = 4$$ $$t_y = -2(1) + 2(1 - 0) = 0$$ $$R_{pivot} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4: Verify by applying to all four vertices.** - $P(1, 1, 1)^T \to (0 - 1 + 4,\ 1 + 0 + 0,\ 1)^T = (3, 1, 1)^T \Rightarrow P' = (3, 1)$ ✓ - $Q(4, 1, 1)^T \to (0 - 1 + 4,\ 4 + 0 + 0,\ 1)^T = (3, 4, 1)^T \Rightarrow Q' = (3, 4)$ ✓ - $R(4, 4, 1)^T \to (0 - 4 + 4,\ 4 + 0 + 0,\ 1)^T = (0, 4, 1)^T \Rightarrow R' = (0, 4)$ ✓ - $S(1, 4, 1)^T \to (0 - 4 + 4,\ 1 + 0 + 0,\ 1)^T = (0, 1, 1)^T \Rightarrow S' = (0, 1)$ ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Beautiful geometric observation:** The new square $P'Q'R'S' = (3,1), (3,4), (0,4), (0,1)$ is the **original square rotated by 90° around its center (2,2)** — which makes perfect sense, because rotating a square by 90° around its own center maps it onto itself (squares have 4-fold rotational symmetry) but with vertices permuted. The new shape is **still a unit square** of side 3, now axis-aligned in a new orientation. This is an excellent self-check on the answer. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the off-diagonal entry of $R(\theta)$.** The $(1,2)$ entry is $-\sin\theta$, **not** $+\sin\theta$. Mixing this up gives the transpose (i.e., CW instead of CCW). Examiners dock **1–2 marks** for this. > > 2. **Order of matrix multiplication in composite pivot rotation.** Many students write $R(\theta) \cdot T(x_r, y_r) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$, which is mathematically wrong. The correct order (for column vectors) is $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ — apply the rightmost matrix first. > > 3. **Failing to convert degrees to radians** when working with irrational trig values (e.g., 30°, 45°, 60°). Always state "$\theta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad" explicitly. > > 4. **Omitting the pivot check.** When the problem says "rotate about point (3, 4)", some students still use the origin-only formula. **Always identify the pivot first**. > > 5. **Sign error in the composite formula translation term.** The term $y_r \sin\theta$ in the $x'$ equation is a classic sign-flip trap. **Re-derive from scratch** if uncertain. > > 6. **Not verifying the determinant.** A rotation matrix must have $\det = 1$. If your matrix has $\det = -1$, you have a **reflection**, not a rotation. This is an instant **2-mark deduction**. > > 7. **Mixing up CW and CCW.** When a problem says "rotate by 30°" without specifying direction, KTU's default is **CCW (positive)**. If the problem says "clockwise", use $\theta = -30°$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Rotation is an isometry** — preserves distance, angle, area, and shape. - [x] **CCW is positive** in KTU / right-handed convention; CW is negative. - [x] The **standard 2×2 rotation matrix** is $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$. - [x] The **CW matrix** is its transpose: $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$. - [x] **Rotation about arbitrary pivot** = Translate-to-origin → Rotate → Translate-back. - [x] The **composite formula** is $x' = x_r + (x-x_r)\cos\theta - (y-y_r)\sin\theta$, $y' = y_r + (x-x_r)\sin\theta + (y-y_r)\cos\theta$. - [x] The **3×3 homogeneous matrix** for rotation about pivot $(x_r, y_r)$ has translation column $(x_r(1-\cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta,\ -x_r\sin\theta + y_r(1-\cos\theta))^T$. - [x] **Inverse rotation** = transpose = rotation by $-\theta$. - [x] **Determinant of $R(\theta)$** = 1 (always) — this is a critical sanity check. - [x] **Special angles** to memorize: $0° \to I$, $90° \to \begin{bmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{bmatrix}$, $180° \to -I$, $270° \to \begin{bmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{bmatrix}$, $360° \to I$. - [x] **Radian conversion**: $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \times \pi/180$. - [x] **Trig values** to memorize: $\cos 30° = \sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\sin 30° = \cos 60° = 1/2$, $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = 1/\sqrt{2}$. - [x] **Order of composition for column vectors**: $T_{\text{back}} \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{to-origin}}$ (apply rightmost first). - [x] **Order of composition for row vectors**: opposite — leftmost applied first. (KTU 2024 default is column vectors.) - [x] **Geometric intuition**: every rotated point lies on a circle of radius $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ centered at the pivot. - [x] **Engineering applications**: CAD, video games, AR/VR, OCR deskewing, medical imaging, satellite georegistration, CNN data augmentation. > [!TIP] > **Final exam-day mantra:** *"Identify the pivot, identify the direction, write the right matrix, multiply in the correct order, verify the determinant is 1, and double-check the signs in the off-diagonal entries."* Following these six steps will earn you full marks on virtually every KTU rotation question.

topics.title: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT602 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Scaling** is a rigid 2D geometric transformation that alters the **size (dimensions)** of a graphical object by multiplying the Cartesian coordinates of every point on the object by **scaling factors** along the X and Y axes independently.

Mathematically, a 2D scaling operation on a point $P(x, y)$ produces a new point $P'(x', y')$ such that:

$$
x' = s_x \cdot x \quad \text{and} \quad y' = s_y \cdot y
$$

where $s_x$ and $s_y$ are the **scaling factors** (also called *scale coefficients*) along the X and Y axes respectively. The scaling transformation is represented in matrix form as:

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix}
$$

In **homogeneous coordinate form** (essential for composing with translation in a single matrix multiplication):

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Scaling, combined with translation, forms the basis of all affine transformations. The homogeneous form $3 \times 3$ matrix is mandatory in your exam answers because KTU valuations require the use of the composite transformation matrix format.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **photograph printed on a rubber sheet**. If you stretch the sheet **horizontally**, the photograph becomes **wider** (length increases) but the height remains the same. If you stretch it **vertically**, it becomes **taller**. If you stretch **both ways equally**, the photograph enlarges **proportionally** (a uniform zoom-in effect).

- **Stretching factor along X** $\rightarrow$ $s_x$ (the X-scaling factor)
- **Stretching factor along Y** $\rightarrow$ $s_y$ (the Y-scaling factor)
- A factor $> 1$ stretches/enlarges
- A factor between $0$ and $1$ shrinks/reduces
- A factor of exactly $1$ leaves that axis unchanged

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think of $s_x$ and $s_y$ as **"zoom multipliers"** for the X and Y axes. A point at $(3, 4)$ with $s_x = 2, s_y = 2$ (uniform zoom of $2\times$) becomes $(6, 8)$ — every coordinate is multiplied by 2.

## 1.3 Classification of Scaling (High-Yield for KTU)

| Scaling Type | Condition | Geometric Effect | Example Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Scaling** | $s_x = s_y$ | Proportional enlargement/reduction | $s_x = s_y = 2$ |
| **Differential (Non-Uniform) Scaling** | $s_x \neq s_y$ | Object becomes distorted/stretched | $s_x = 2, s_y = 1$ |
| **Enlargement** | $s_x > 1$ and $s_y > 1$ | Object grows in size | $s_x = s_y = 3$ |
| **Reduction** | $0 < s_x < 1$ and $0 < s_y < 1$ | Object shrinks | $s_x = s_y = 0.5$ |
| **Reflection through origin** | $s_x = s_y = -1$ | Equivalent to $180°$ rotation | $s_x = s_y = -1$ |
| **Reflection across X-axis** | $s_x = 1, s_y = -1$ | Mirror image (vertical flip) | $s_x = 1, s_y = -1$ |
| **Reflection across Y-axis** | $s_x = -1, s_y = 1$ | Mirror image (horizontal flip) | $s_x = -1, s_y = 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A **negative scale factor** produces a **reflection** combined with scaling. This is a frequent KTU exam trap — students forget the reflection component.

## 1.4 Fixed-Point Scaling

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Module 2 Emphasis):** Scaling is performed about a reference point called the **Fixed Point** $P_f(x_f, y_f)$, which remains invariant (does not move) under the transformation.

A pure origin-based scaling moves the object away from its visual position. To keep an object anchored (e.g., scaling a button around its center), we use **fixed-point scaling**. This is achieved by a 3-step composite transformation:

1. **Translate** the fixed point to the origin: $T(-x_f, -y_f)$
2. **Scale** about the origin: $S(s_x, s_y)$
3. **Translate** back: $T(x_f, y_f)$

The composite transformation matrix is:

$$
M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Scaling

Scaling operates on the **vertex coordinates** of an object. For a polygon with $n$ vertices $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$, the scaled polygon has vertices $\{(x_i', y_i')\}_{i=1}^{n}$ where:

$$
x_i' = s_x \cdot x_i, \quad y_i' = s_y \cdot y_i
$$

The operation is applied **pointwise** to every vertex. Lines, curves, and filled regions are scaled by transforming their defining control points.

### Why Scaling Works Geometrically

- Each point $P(x, y)$ is treated as a **position vector** $\vec{p} = (x, y)^T$ from the origin.
- Multiplying by $S$ scales the **length** of the vector by the corresponding factor along each axis.
- The origin $(0, 0)$ is the only point that remains invariant under pure origin-based scaling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they are the **backbone of every 2D scaling problem** in KTU exams.

| # | Transformation | Matrix Form | Coordinate Equations |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scaling about origin** | $\begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = s_x \cdot x, \quad y' = s_y \cdot y$ |
| 2 | **Scaling about fixed point** $\left(x_f, y_f\right)$ | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | $x' = x_f + s_x(x - x_f), \quad y' = y_f + s_y(y - y_f)$ |
| 3 | **Inverse scaling** | $\begin{pmatrix} 1/s_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/s_y \end{pmatrix}$ | $x = x'/s_x, \quad y = y'/s_y$ |
| 4 | **Uniform scaling** $\left(s_x = s_y = s\right)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = s \cdot x, \quad y' = s \cdot y$ |
| 5 | **Reflection (X-axis)** | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = x, \quad y' = -y$ |
| 6 | **Reflection (Y-axis)** | $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = -x, \quad y' = y$ |
| 7 | **Reflection (origin)** | $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = -x, \quad y' = -y$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on Inverse:** The inverse of scaling is defined only when $s_x \neq 0$ and $s_y \neq 0$. If either is zero, the transformation is **singular** (maps a 2D plane to a 1D line or a point) and is **non-invertible**.

## 2.3 Composite Matrix for Fixed-Point Scaling (Detailed)

Starting with the 3 matrices in homogeneous form:

$$
T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

First, compute the intermediate matrix $S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$
S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Then pre-multiply by $T(x_f, y_f)$:

$$
M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Applying this matrix to a point $P(x, y, 1)$ gives:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Engineers scale mechanical parts (zoom-in to view details, zoom-out to see assembly fit).
- **Medical Imaging:** Radiologists enlarge MRI/CT scan regions of interest to study pathologies.
- **Cartography (Maps):** Zoom levels in Google Maps are non-uniform scalings of the geographic projection.
- **UI/UX Design:** Resizing icons, buttons, and screen elements based on user device DPI.
- **3D Printing & CNC:** Pre-fabrication scaling to account for material shrinkage or thermal expansion.
- **Animation & Gaming:** Real-time scaling of sprites and 3D models (player growth/shrink power-ups).

## 2.5 Effect of Scaling on Geometric Properties

| Geometric Property | Effect Under Uniform Scaling $\left(s_x = s_y = s\right)$ | Effect Under Non-Uniform Scaling $\left(s_x \neq s_y\right)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Length of a line** | Multiplied by $s$ | Different scaling along axes |
| **Area of a polygon** | Multiplied by $s^2$ | Multiplied by $s_x \cdot s_y$ |
| **Angle between lines** | **Preserved** | **NOT preserved** (distortion) |
| **Parallelism** | **Preserved** | **Preserved** |
| **Straightness** | **Preserved** | **Preserved** |
| **Shape (similarity)** | **Preserved** | **NOT preserved** |
| **Orientation** | **Preserved** (if $s > 0$) | **Preserved** (if both $s > 0$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Uniform scaling is a **similarity transformation** (preserves shape); non-uniform scaling is **NOT** a similarity transformation (it distorts shape — a circle becomes an ellipse).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scaling Matrix from Coordinate Equations

**Starting Point:** We want a $2 \times 2$ matrix $S$ such that for any point $P(x, y)$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = S \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}
$$

From the defining equations of scaling:
- $x' = s_x \cdot x + 0 \cdot y$
- $y' = 0 \cdot x + s_y \cdot y$

**Step 1:** Write the equations in matrix form.

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the transformation matrix.

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (Homogeneous form):** To allow composition with translation, we extend to $3 \times 3$:

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The third row $(0, 0, 1)$ ensures the homogeneous coordinate remains $1$ after transformation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Matrix for Fixed-Point Scaling

**Goal:** Derive the composite matrix for scaling about a fixed point $\left(x_f, y_f\right)$.

**Step 1: Translate the fixed point to the origin.**

This moves the object so that the fixed point is at $(0, 0)$:

$$
T_1 = T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Apply scaling about the new origin.**

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Translate the fixed point back to its original position.**

$$
T_2 = T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the composite matrix $M = T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1$.**

First, compute $S \cdot T_1$:

$$
S \cdot T_1 = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Now compute $M = T_2 \cdot (S \cdot T_1)$:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Perform the matrix multiplication element by element.**

Row 1 of $T_2$ times each column of $(S \cdot T_1)$:

- $M_{11} = (1)(s_x) + (0)(0) + (x_f)(0) = s_x$
- $M_{12} = (1)(0) + (0)(s_y) + (x_f)(0) = 0$
- $M_{13} = (1)(-s_x x_f) + (0)(-s_y y_f) + (x_f)(1) = -s_x x_f + x_f = x_f(1 - s_x)$

Row 2 of $T_2$ times each column of $(S \cdot T_1)$:

- $M_{21} = (0)(s_x) + (1)(0) + (y_f)(0) = 0$
- $M_{22} = (0)(0) + (1)(s_y) + (y_f)(0) = s_y$
- $M_{23} = (0)(-s_x x_f) + (1)(-s_y y_f) + (y_f)(1) = -s_y y_f + y_f = y_f(1 - s_y)$

Row 3 of $T_2$ times each column of $(S \cdot T_1)$:

- $M_{31} = 0, \quad M_{32} = 0, \quad M_{33} = 1$

**Step 6: Write the final composite matrix.**

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 7: Apply $M$ to a point $P(x, y, 1)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned} x' &= s_x \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + x_f(1 - s_x) \\ &= s_x \cdot x + x_f - s_x \cdot x_f \\ &= x_f + s_x(x - x_f) \end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned} y' &= 0 \cdot x + s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y) \\ &= s_y \cdot y + y_f - s_y \cdot y_f \\ &= y_f + s_y(y - y_f) \end{aligned}
$$

These are the standard fixed-point scaling equations used in every KTU exam.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Problem Type:** "Scale a triangle with vertices A(1, 1), B(5, 1), C(3, 6) by factors $s_x = 2, s_y = 3$ about the fixed point $(2, 2)$."

**Step 1:** Use the composite matrix:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2(1 - 2) \\ 0 & 3 & 2(1 - 3) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 3 & -4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Transform vertex A(1, 1):

$$
A' = M \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2(1) + 0(1) + (-2)(1) \\ 0(1) + 3(1) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So $A' = (0, -1)$.

**Step 3:** Transform vertex B(5, 1):

$$
B' = M \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2(5) + 0(1) + (-2)(1) \\ 0(5) + 3(1) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So $B' = (8, -1)$.

**Step 4:** Transform vertex C(3, 6):

$$
C' = M \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2(3) + 0(6) + (-2)(1) \\ 0(3) + 3(6) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 14 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So $C' = (4, 14)$.

**Step 5:** Final scaled triangle vertices: $A'(0, -1)$, $B'(8, -1)$, $C'(4, 14)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Trick:** Substitute the fixed point $(2, 2)$ itself — it should map to itself. $x' = 2 + 2(2 - 2) = 2$, $y' = 2 + 3(2 - 2) = 2$. ✓ Confirmed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Scaling Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputPoint[Original Point P x y]
    inputMatrix[Scaling Matrix S]
    multiply[Matrix Multiplication]
    outputPoint[Transformed Point P prime x prime y prime]
    shape[Polygon Vertices List]
    shapeOp[Apply S to Each Vertex]
    shapeOut[Scaled Polygon]

    inputPoint --> multiply
    inputMatrix --> multiply
    multiply --> outputPoint

    shape --> shapeOp
    inputMatrix --> shapeOp
    shapeOp --> shapeOut
```

## 4.2 Fixed-Point Scaling Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Step1["Step 1: Translate Fixed Point to Origin"]
        T1["Translation T minus xf minus yf"]
    end

    subgraph Step2["Step 2: Apply Origin-Based Scaling"]
        S1["Scaling Matrix S sx sy"]
    end

    subgraph Step3["Step 3: Translate Back to Original Position"]
        T2["Translation T plus xf plus yf"]
    end

    Input["Original Point P x y"] --> T1
    T1 --> S1
    S1 --> T2
    T2 --> Output["Scaled Point P prime x prime y prime"]

    composite["Composite Matrix M = T2 dot S dot T1"] -.Computed from.-> T1
    composite -.Computed from.-> S1
    composite -.Computed from.-> T2
```

## 4.3 Geometric Effect of Scaling (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Origin["Scaling About Origin"]
        obj1["Square at x in 1 to 3, y in 1 to 3"]
        sMatrix["S equals diag of sx, sy"]
        obj1Out["Scaled Square"]

        obj1 --> sMatrix
        sMatrix --> obj1Out
    end

    subgraph FixedPt["Scaling About Fixed Point"]
        obj2["Square at x in 1 to 3, y in 1 to 3"]
        fixedPt["Fixed Point P fixed equals 2, 2"]
        sMatrix2["Composite M equals T dot S dot T inverse"]
        obj2Out["Scaled Square Anchored at Fixed Point"]

        obj2 --> sMatrix2
        fixedPt --> sMatrix2
        sMatrix2 --> obj2Out
    end

    Origin --> Compare
    FixedPt --> Compare
    Compare["Comparison: Origin-based scaling translates object; Fixed-point scaling keeps anchor invariant"]
```

## 4.4 Coordinate Space Transformation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Before["Before Scaling: s_x equals 2, s_y equals 3"]
        P1["P equals 1, 1"]
        P2["Q equals 3, 2"]
    end

    subgraph After["After Scaling About Origin"]
        P1Out["P prime equals 2, 3"]
        P2Out["Q prime equals 6, 6"]
    end

    P1 -->|multiply by S| P1Out
    P2 -->|multiply by S| P2Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram above illustrates that under scaling $s_x = 2, s_y = 3$, every X-coordinate is doubled and every Y-coordinate is tripled. The origin $(0, 0)$ remains fixed, and the object moves outward in both axes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define 2D scaling transformation. Write its transformation matrix and explain the effect of negative scale factors.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** 2D scaling is a transformation that changes the size of an object by multiplying the X and Y coordinates of every point by scaling factors $s_x$ and $s_y$ respectively.

**Transformation Matrix (homogeneous form):**

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Effect of negative scale factors:**

- If $s_x < 0$: object is reflected about the Y-axis and scaled.
- If $s_y < 0$: object is reflected about the X-axis and scaled.
- If both $s_x < 0$ and $s_y < 0$: equivalent to $180°$ rotation about the origin (two reflections cancel out, but the object is scaled by $\vert s_x \vert \cdot \vert s_y \vert$).

**Coordinate Equations:** $x' = s_x \cdot x$, $y' = s_y \cdot y$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between uniform and non-uniform scaling with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Uniform Scaling | Non-Uniform Scaling |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition** | $s_x = s_y$ | $s_x \neq s_y$ |
| **Shape** | Preserved (similarity transformation) | Distorted |
| **Example 1** | $s_x = s_y = 2$: doubles the size of object | $s_x = 2, s_y = 1$: doubles width only |
| **Example 2** | $s_x = s_y = 0.5$: halves the size | $s_x = 1, s_y = 3$: triples height only |
| **Use Case** | Zoom in/out of a photograph | Image stretching, text italicization |
| **Area Scaling Factor** | $s^2$ | $s_x \cdot s_y$ |

A circle remains a circle under uniform scaling but becomes an ellipse under non-uniform scaling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the composite transformation matrix to scale an object about a fixed point $\left(x_f, y_f\right)$ by scaling factors $s_x$ and $s_y$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1), B(6, 3), C(4, 8)$. Apply a scaling transformation with $s_x = 3, s_y = 2$ about the fixed point $(4, 4)$. Compute the new coordinates and plot/draw the resulting triangle. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1:** A fixed-point scaling is composed of three sequential operations:
1. Translate fixed point to origin: $T(-x_f, -y_f)$
2. Scale about origin: $S(s_x, s_y)$
3. Translate fixed point back: $T(x_f, y_f)$

**Step 2:** Express each in homogeneous matrix form:

$$
T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$
S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the composite $M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply to point $P(x, y, 1)$:

$$
x' = s_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - s_x) = x_f + s_x(x - x_f)
$$
$$
y' = s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y) = y_f + s_y(y - y_f)
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Substitute $s_x = 3, s_y = 2, x_f = 4, y_f = 4$:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 & 4(1 - 3) \\ 0 & 2 & 4(1 - 2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 & -8 \\ 0 & 2 & -4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Transform $A(2, 1)$:

$$
A' = \begin{pmatrix} 3(2) + 0(1) + (-8)(1) \\ 0(2) + 2(1) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$A' = (-2, -2)$.

**Step 3:** Transform $B(6, 3)$:

$$
B' = \begin{pmatrix} 3(6) + 0(3) + (-8)(1) \\ 0(6) + 2(3) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$B' = (10, 2)$.

**Step 4:** Transform $C(4, 8)$:

$$
C' = \begin{pmatrix} 3(4) + 0(8) + (-8)(1) \\ 0(4) + 2(8) + (-4)(1) \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 12 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$C' = (4, 12)$.

**Step 5: Verification:** Fixed point $(4, 4)$ maps to $(4, 4)$ ✓.

**Final Scaled Triangle:** $A'(-2, -2), B'(10, 2), C'(4, 12)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of fixed-point scaling. Derive the equations $x' = x_f + s_x(x - x_f)$ and $y' = y_f + s_y(y - y_f)$ for scaling about a fixed point. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Given a unit square with vertices $P_1(1, 1), P_2(3, 1), P_3(3, 3), P_4(1, 3)$, perform the following sequence of transformations: (i) scale by $s_x = 2, s_y = 3$ about the origin, (ii) then translate by $(5, 4)$. Find the final coordinates of the square's vertices. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Concept:** Fixed-point scaling is a scaling operation where one specific point $\left(x_f, y_f\right)$ of the object (called the *fixed point*) remains stationary during the transformation, while all other points move relative to it. This is useful when the object must be scaled "in place" without being displaced.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Translate so that fixed point coincides with origin. The point $P(x, y)$ becomes $P_1(x - x_f, y - y_f)$.

**Step 2:** Apply origin-based scaling:

$$
x_2 = s_x(x - x_f), \quad y_2 = s_y(y - y_f)
$$

**Step 3:** Translate back to restore the original position of the fixed point:

$$
x' = x_2 + x_f = s_x(x - x_f) + x_f = x_f + s_x(x - x_f)
$$
$$
y' = y_2 + y_f = s_y(y - y_f) + y_f = y_f + s_y(y - y_f)
$$

These are the required fixed-point scaling equations.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Form the scaling matrix:

$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Form the translation matrix:

$$
T = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Composite matrix $M = T \cdot S$:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply to each vertex.

- $P_1(1, 1)$: $P_1' = (2(1) + 5, \; 3(1) + 4) = (7, 7)$
- $P_2(3, 1)$: $P_2' = (2(3) + 5, \; 3(1) + 4) = (11, 7)$
- $P_3(3, 3)$: $P_3' = (2(3) + 5, \; 3(3) + 4) = (11, 13)$
- $P_4(1, 3)$: $P_4' = (2(1) + 5, \; 3(3) + 4) = (7, 13)$

**Final Square Vertices:** $P_1'(7, 7), P_2'(11, 7), P_3'(11, 13), P_4'(7, 13)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Order of Multiplication:** Students often write $S \cdot T$ instead of $T \cdot S$ for "scale then translate." In **column-vector convention** (KTU standard), the rightmost matrix is applied first, so the composite is $T \cdot S$. Reversing the order will produce incorrect answers and lose up to **5 marks**.
> 2. **Missing Homogeneous Coordinate:** Failing to write the $3 \times 3$ matrix with the third row $(0, 0, 1)$ loses 1 mark even if the answer is correct.
> 3. **Forgetting Fixed-Point Check:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for verifying that the fixed point maps to itself. Skipping this verification is a common mark-loss.
> 4. **Sign Errors in $x_f(1 - s_x)$:** When $s_x > 1$, this term is **negative**. Students often write $+x_f(s_x - 1)$ (algebraically identical but causes confusion if the algebra is not properly expanded).
> 5. **Confusing Reflection with Scaling:** A negative $s_x$ means scaling **plus reflection**. Do not interpret it as pure reflection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Definition:** Scaling alters size via $x' = s_x \cdot x$, $y' = s_y \cdot y$ about the origin.
- **Matrix (2×2):** $S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{pmatrix}$ — extend to **3×3 homogeneous form** for KTU.
- **Homogeneous Form:** Third row is always $(0, 0, 1)$.
- **Uniform vs Non-Uniform:** $s_x = s_y$ (shape preserved) vs $s_x \neq s_y$ (shape distorted).
- **Fixed-Point Equations:** $x' = x_f + s_x(x - x_f)$, $y' = y_f + s_y(y - y_f)$.
- **Composite Matrix (Fixed-Point):** $M = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
- **Pipeline Order:** Translate to origin $\rightarrow$ Scale $\rightarrow$ Translate back.
- **Negative Scale Factor = Reflection + Scaling:** $s = -1$ is a reflection.
- **Inverse:** $S^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/s_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/s_y \end{pmatrix}$ (only if $s_x, s_y \neq 0$).
- **Area Scaling Factor:** $s_x \cdot s_y$ (general) or $s^2$ (uniform).
- **Angles Preserved:** Only under uniform scaling with $s > 0$.
- **Origin-Invariance:** Only the origin $(0, 0)$ is invariant under pure origin-based scaling.
- **Always verify** that the fixed point maps to itself after transformation (1 mark reserved).
- **Column-Vector Convention** is the KTU standard — read matrices **right to left** for application order.
- **Always use $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrices** in your KTU answer sheet, even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection and Shearing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCNT602 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflection and Shearing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reflection — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reflection** is a rigid-body geometric transformation that produces a **mirror image** of a 2D/3D object across a designated line (called the **axis of reflection** or **mirror line**) or a designated point. It is an **isometric** transformation because the size, shape, and area of the object remain unchanged; only the orientation is reversed. The reflection axis acts exactly like a physical mirror, where every point of the object is mapped to its mirror twin at an equal perpendicular distance on the opposite side.

Mathematically, reflection is represented by an **orthogonal transformation matrix** $M$ such that $M^T M = I$ (i.e., it is a pure rotation/reflection) and $\det(M) = -1$ (the negative determinant distinguishes a *proper* reflection from a rotation, where $\det = +1$).

### 1.2 Shearing — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shearing** is a non-rigid linear transformation that **distorts** the geometry of an object by sliding its points parallel to a chosen axis, where the displacement (shear factor) is **proportional to the signed distance** of the point from a fixed reference line. It preserves areas (in 2D) and volumes (in 3D) but **changes the shape** — converting a rectangle into a parallelogram, for instance. The shear factor is dimensionless and is denoted $sh_x$ (for shearing along the x-direction) or $sh_y$ (for shearing along the y-direction).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Reflection — The Bathroom Mirror Analogy:**
Imagine the word **"GRAPHICS"** written on a piece of transparent glass. Hold it up to a real bathroom mirror. The image you see is the **reflected** version. The mirror is the *axis of reflection*. The original letter "G" appears on the right side of the original, but in the mirror, the G flips to the left side. The **handedness** (chirality) is reversed — a right-hand glove becomes a left-hand glove. This is precisely what reflection does in computer graphics: it produces the **mirror twin** of the input object.

**Shearing — The Italicizing & Wind Analogy:**
Open Microsoft Word and select a letter, then press the *Italic* button (Ctrl+I). The letter slants to the right. This slant is a **horizontal shear**. Another analogy: imagine a tall rectangular building made of stacked horizontal floors during a strong wind blowing sideways. Each floor slides a little more than the one below it. The bottom floor stays put, the top floor slides the most, and the result is a **slanted parallelogram** — a shear transformation. The amount each floor slides is governed by the **shear factor** $sh_x$.

### 1.4 Classification Summary

| Transformation | Type | Determinant | Area Preserved? | Handedness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reflection (about a line) | Rigid (isometry) | $-1$ | Yes | Reversed |
| Shear (x or y direction) | Non-rigid (affine) | $+1$ | Yes | Preserved |
| Identity | Rigid | $+1$ | Yes | Preserved |
| Rotation | Rigid | $+1$ | Yes | Preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 syllabus explicitly lists *reflection and shearing* alongside translation, rotation, and scaling under **2D transformations**. Students are expected to derive the transformation matrices, apply them to given points, and compute composite transformations using **homogeneous coordinates** (a $3 \times 3$ matrix framework that unifies linear transformations and translations into a single matrix product).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reflection — Operational Logic

Reflection is conceptually a "two-step" process: **1)** drop a perpendicular from the point to the axis, **2)** extend the perpendicular an equal distance on the other side. The axis of reflection is the **perpendicular bisector** of the line segment joining a point and its image.

For the case of reflecting point $P(x, y)$ across the **x-axis**:
- The y-coordinate is negated (it flips across the horizontal line $y = 0$).
- The x-coordinate remains untouched.
- Therefore: $P'(x', y') = (x, -y)$.

The corresponding **transformation matrix in homogeneous coordinates** is:

$$
M_{ref,x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The same conceptual logic applies to every other axis. The matrix is built by looking at the image of the **basis vectors** $\hat{i} = (1, 0)$ and $\hat{j} = (0, 1)$.

### 2.2 Shearing — Operational Logic

In a **x-shear** (shear parallel to the x-axis), every point is displaced horizontally by an amount that is a linear function of its y-coordinate. The reference line is the **x-axis** itself ($y = 0$), and points on this line are not moved. The transformation is:

$$
x' = x + sh_x \cdot y
$$

$$
y' = y
$$

The parameter $sh_x$ is the **shear factor** — it is the horizontal displacement of a point at unit distance ($y = 1$) from the x-axis. If $sh_x = 2$, then a point at $y = 1$ is shifted 2 units to the right; a point at $y = 3$ is shifted 6 units to the right.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why shearing preserves area:** The shear matrix has determinant $= 1 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot sh_x = 1$. By the geometric interpretation of the determinant as the **signed area scaling factor** of the linear map, $\det = 1$ implies the area of any shape is preserved — a unit square becomes a unit parallelogram.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Transformation | Transformation Matrix (Homogeneous, $3 \times 3$) | Resulting Coordinates |
|---|---|---|
| Reflection about **x-axis** ($y = 0$) | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, -y)$ |
| Reflection about **y-axis** ($x = 0$) | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-x, y)$ |
| Reflection about **origin** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-x, -y)$ |
| Reflection about line **$y = x$** | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(y, x)$ |
| Reflection about line **$y = -x$** | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-y, -x)$ |
| Reflection about line **$y = mx + c$** | Composite: Translate(0, $-c$) $\to$ Rotate($-\theta$) $\to$ Reflect(x-axis) $\to$ Rotate($\theta$) $\to$ Translate(0, $c$) | See derivation below |
| **x-shear** (about x-axis) | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_x & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x + sh_x y,\ y)$ |
| **y-shear** (about y-axis) | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_y & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x,\ y + sh_y x)$ |
| **x-shear with ref. line $y = y_{ref}$** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_x & 1 & -sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x + sh_x(y - y_{ref}),\ y)$ |
| **y-shear with ref. line $x = x_{ref}$** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_y & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -sh_y \cdot x_{ref} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x,\ y + sh_y(x - x_{ref}))$ |

Where:
- $sh_x$ = **shear factor in x-direction** (dimensionless scalar).
- $sh_y$ = **shear factor in y-direction** (dimensionless scalar).
- $y_{ref}$, $x_{ref}$ = **reference line coordinates** (the line that remains stationary during shearing).
- $\theta = \arctan(m)$ is the angle the line $y = mx + c$ makes with the x-axis.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reflection** is fundamental in **computer-aided design (CAD)** for creating symmetric parts (e.g., car body panels, turbine blades) where engineers design one half and mirror it. It is also used in **planar symmetry detection** in medical imaging (MRI/CT scans) and in **mirror-based optical simulations** for camera design.
- **Shearing** is essential in **font rendering** (italic text), in **3D projection** (oblique/cabinet projections where one face is sheared), in **image warping** for special effects in animation, and in **finite element analysis** to model material deformation under shear stress (e.g., earthquake engineering, metal forging).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Reflection About the x-axis

**Goal:** Derive the matrix $M_{ref,x}$ that maps any point $P(x, y)$ to its mirror image $P'(x', y')$ across the x-axis.

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.**
The x-axis is the horizontal line $y = 0$. A point $P(x, y)$ above the x-axis (where $y > 0$) has a mirror twin $P'(x, -y)$ below the x-axis. The x-axis is the **perpendicular bisector** of segment $PP'$.

**Step 2 — Identify the new coordinates directly from geometry.**
The horizontal distance from the y-axis is unchanged, so $x' = x$. The vertical distance from the x-axis is negated, so $y' = -y$.

$$
P'(x', y') = (x, -y)
$$

**Step 3 — Express this as a matrix equation in homogeneous coordinates.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify by basis vector analysis.**
Where does $(1, 0)$ go? It lies ON the x-axis, so it must map to itself: $(1, 0, 1)^T$. Plugging in: $(1, 0, 1)^T \to (1, 0, 1)^T$. ✓
Where does $(0, 1)$ go? It is 1 unit above the x-axis, so it must flip to 1 unit below: $(0, -1)$. Plugging in: $(0, 1, 1)^T \to (0, -1, 1)^T$. ✓

This confirms the matrix is correctly derived.

### 3.2 Derivation of Reflection About the Line $y = x$

**Step 1 — Use the basis vector method.**
The line $y = x$ makes a 45° angle with the x-axis. We ask: where do the basis vectors go?
- $\hat{i} = (1, 0)$: This point lies on the x-axis. To reflect it across the line $y = x$, draw the perpendicular from $(1, 0)$ to the line $y = x$ — the foot is at $(0.5, 0.5)$. The mirror image is at $(0, 1)$.
- $\hat{j} = (0, 1)$: By symmetry, this maps to $(1, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Place the images of basis vectors as columns of the transformation matrix.**

$$
M_{ref,y=x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to a general point $(x, y)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned} x' &= 0 \cdot x + 1 \cdot y + 0 \cdot 1 = y \\ y' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 0 \cdot 1 = x \end{aligned}
$$

So $P'(x', y') = (y, x)$. The x and y coordinates are **swapped**. This is the geometric definition of reflection about $y = x$ — it interchanges the two axes. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation of Reflection About an Arbitrary Line $y = mx + c$

This is a **high-yield KTU problem**. The strategy is to use a sequence of simpler transformations that converts the general case into the canonical "reflection about the x-axis" case.

**Step 1 — Translate to bring the line through the origin.**
The line $y = mx + c$ has y-intercept $c$. Translate the entire scene by $(0, -c)$ so the line becomes $y = mx$ (passing through origin). Translation matrix:

$$
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotate to align the line with the x-axis.**
The line $y = mx$ makes an angle $\theta = \arctan(m)$ with the x-axis. Rotate the scene by $-\theta$ so the line coincides with the x-axis. Rotation matrix (for rotation by angle $\alpha$ counter-clockwise):

$$
R(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha & 0 \\ \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For clockwise rotation by $\theta$ (which is counter-clockwise by $-\theta$):

$$
R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Reflect about the x-axis** (the canonical case).

$$
M_{ref,x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Undo the rotation (rotate back by $+\theta$).**

$$
R(+\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Undo the translation (translate back by $(0, +c)$).**

$$
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compose all five transformations in correct order.**
The composite matrix is the product (note: in homogeneous coordinates, the rightmost matrix is applied first):

$$
M = T_2 \cdot R(\theta) \cdot M_{ref,x} \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot T_1
$$

This 5-step pipeline can be drawn as: **Translate Down → Rotate Back → Reflect → Rotate Forward → Translate Up**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Multiplication is CRITICAL.** Matrix multiplication is **non-commutative** ($AB \neq BA$). The transformations must be applied in the order: first the rightmost matrix to the point column vector, then the next one to the left, and so on. KTU examiners frequently award full 14 marks only if the order is precisely correct.

### 3.4 Derivation of x-Shear with Reference Line $y = y_{ref}$

**Step 1 — Set up the shear transformation equations.**
An x-shear with reference line $y = y_{ref}$ means points ON the line $y = y_{ref}$ are not moved. Points above the line slide right (if $sh_x > 0$) by an amount proportional to their distance above the line.

$$
x' = x + sh_x \cdot (y - y_{ref})
$$

$$
y' = y
$$

**Step 2 — Express in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_x & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y - y_{ref} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \text{(with } y_{ref} \text{ adjustment in third column)}
$$

To absorb the $-sh_x \cdot y_{ref}$ term properly, we write:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_x & 1 & -sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify by testing a point on the reference line.**
Test $P(x, y_{ref})$:

$$
\begin{aligned} x' &= x + 0 \cdot y_{ref} - sh_x \cdot y_{ref} = x - sh_x \cdot y_{ref} + sh_x \cdot y_{ref} = x \\ y' &= sh_x \cdot x + y_{ref} \end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this gives $y' = sh_x \cdot x + y_{ref}$, not $y_{ref}$. This is incorrect for a "point on the reference line" because y is also being affected. The correct interpretation is that the y-coordinate is **unchanged** by the shear, so we must keep $y' = y$ in the matrix row 2 column 2:

The **corrected matrix** for x-shear about line $y = y_{ref}$ is:

$$
M_{shx, yref} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_x & 1 & -sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

And applying it to a point on the reference line $y = y_{ref}$:

$$
\begin{aligned} x' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y_{ref} + 0 = x \\ y' &= sh_x \cdot x + 1 \cdot y_{ref} - sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \end{aligned}
$$

The y-coordinate is NOT preserved unless the point has $x = 0$. So the proper convention is to **subtract $sh_x \cdot y_{ref}$ from $x'$ alone**, keeping $y' = y$. The matrix is:

$$
M_{shx, yref} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (identity) } + sh_x \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -y_{ref} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

which simplifies to:

$$
M_{shx, yref} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & -sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply to point $(x, y_{ref})$:

$$
x' = x + sh_x \cdot y_{ref} - sh_x \cdot y_{ref} = x \quad \text{(unchanged ✓)}
$$

$$
y' = y_{ref} \quad \text{(unchanged ✓)}
$$

**Step 4 — Final verified matrix.**

$$
\boxed{M_{shx, yref} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & -sh_x \cdot y_{ref} \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Composite Shear + Reflection

**Problem:** Given triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$. Apply the following sequence of transformations and find the new coordinates:
1. Reflect about the line $y = x$.
2. Apply x-shear with $sh_x = 2$ about the x-axis.
3. Translate by $(3, -1)$.

**Step 1 — Reflection about $y = x$:** Swaps coordinates.
$A \to A_1(1, 2)$, $B \to B_1(1, 4)$, $C \to C_1(4, 3)$.

**Step 2 — x-shear with $sh_x = 2$:** Use $x' = x + 2y$, $y' = y$.
$A_1(1, 2) \to A_2(1 + 4, 2) = A_2(5, 2)$.
$B_1(1, 4) \to B_2(1 + 8, 4) = B_2(9, 4)$.
$C_1(4, 3) \to C_2(4 + 6, 3) = C_2(10, 3)$.

**Step 3 — Translation by $(3, -1)$:** Add $(3, -1)$ to each.
$A_2(5, 2) \to A'(8, 1)$.
$B_2(9, 4) \to B'(12, 3)$.
$C_2(10, 3) \to C'(13, 2)$.

**Step 4 — Composite matrix verification.**

$$
M = T(3,-1) \cdot Sh_x(2) \cdot Ref_{y=x}
$$

$$
M_{Ref} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad M_{Shx} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad M_{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

First compute $M_{Shx} \cdot M_{Ref}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now compute $M = M_T \cdot (M_{Shx} \cdot M_{Ref})$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply to $A(2, 1, 1)^T$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 + 1 + 3 \\ 2 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait — this gives $A' = (14, 1)$ but our manual calculation gave $A' = (8, 1)$. The discrepancy indicates a **matrix multiplication order error**. In homogeneous coordinates, when we apply $M_1$ **then** $M_2$ to a point, the combined matrix is $M_2 \cdot M_1$ (i.e., the order of multiplication is the **reverse** of the order of application). The correct composite is:

$$
M = M_T \cdot M_{Shx} \cdot M_{Ref} \quad \text{(applied in order: Ref, then Shx, then T)}
$$

Let me redo: apply to $A(2, 1)$:
- After Ref: $(1, 2)$.
- After Shx with $sh_x = 2$: $(1 + 2 \cdot 2, 2) = (5, 2)$. ✓
- After T(3, -1): $(5 + 3, 2 - 1) = (8, 1)$. ✓

Our manual answer $A' = (8, 1)$ is correct. The matrix computation above had a multiplication error: I should have multiplied $M_T \cdot M_{Shx}$ first, then by $M_{Ref}$.

Let me redo the matrix product correctly:
$M_{Shx} \cdot M_{Ref}$ was already computed as $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ ✓ (this represents: apply Ref first, then Shx).

Then $M_T \cdot (M_{Shx} \cdot M_{Ref})$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1, Col 1: $1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 0 = 2$.
Row 1, Col 2: $1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 0 = 1$.
Row 1, Col 3: $1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 1 = 3$.
Row 2, Col 1: $0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 + (-1) \cdot 0 = 1$.
Row 2, Col 2: $0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + (-1) \cdot 0 = 0$.
Row 2, Col 3: $0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0 + (-1) \cdot 1 = -1$.

So $M = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. (My earlier matrix was wrong.)

Apply to $A(2, 1, 1)^T$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 + 1 + 3 \\ 2 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$A' = (8, 1)$ ✓ — **matches our manual answer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Matrix Rule (KTU Favourite):** If transformations $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$ are applied **in that order** to a point, the combined transformation matrix is $M_{combined} = M_n \cdot M_{n-1} \cdots M_2 \cdot M_1$. The rightmost matrix acts on the point first. Many students lose marks by reversing this order.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Reflection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Point P x comma y] --> B{Choose Axis}
    B --> C[Reflect about x axis]
    B --> D[Reflect about y axis]
    B --> E[Reflect about y equals x]
    B --> F[Reflect about origin]
    C --> G[Apply Matrix M ref x]
    D --> H[Apply Matrix M ref y]
    E --> I[Apply Matrix M ref yeqx]
    F --> J[Apply Matrix M ref origin]
    G --> K[Output P prime]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — General Reflection About Arbitrary Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Original Point P] --> T1[Step 1: Translate by 0 minus c]
    T1 --> R1[Step 2: Rotate by minus theta]
    R1 --> RX[Step 3: Reflect about x axis canonical]
    RX --> R2[Step 4: Rotate by plus theta]
    R2 --> T2[Step 5: Translate by 0 plus c]
    T2 --> Pout[Reflected Point P dash]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Shearing Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Input Object] --> S1{Choose Shear Direction}
    S1 --> S2[X Shear parallel to x axis]
    S1 --> S3[Y Shear parallel to y axis]
    S2 --> S4[Shift each point by shx times y]
    S3 --> S5[Shift each point by shy times x]
    S4 --> S6[Reference line y equals yref unchanged]
    S5 --> S7[Reference line x equals xref unchanged]
    S6 --> Sout[Output Sheared Object]
    S7 --> Sout
```

### 4.4 Composite Transformation Pipeline (KTU Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Input Polygon] --> M1[Transformation M1: Reflection]
    M1 --> M2[Transformation M2: Shearing]
    M2 --> M3[Transformation M3: Translation]
    M3 --> Mc[Composite Matrix Mc equals M3 times M2 times M1]
    Mc --> Pn[Final Transformed Polygon]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The rightmost transformation $M_1$ is applied to the point first (conceptually), but mathematically it is the **leftmost factor** in the product $M_{combined} = M_n \cdots M_1$. The composite matrix $M_c$ can be precomputed and then applied to every vertex of the polygon in a single matrix-vector multiplication, which is computationally efficient for graphics pipelines.
